source.paper: Univ. of Kufa · Formative Block 1

questions.stem: A 48-year-old DIABETIC female with GRADUAL bilateral blurring of vision. Fundus imaging reveals MACULAR THICKENING and EXUDATES.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Centre-involving DIABETIC MACULAR OEDEMA. Anti-VEGF (ranibizumab/aflibercept) is first-line for centre-involving DMO, reducing vascular leak and macular thickness.  Why not the others B/C Decongestant / antibiotic drops have no role in retinal disease. D Aciclovir treats herpetic keratitis, not maculopathy.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Orbital (blow-out) floor fracture. Conservative care = NASAL DECONGESTANTS, oral antibiotic prophylaxis (sinus communication), AVOID NOSE-BLOWING (prevents orbital emphysema), and maxillofacial referral; surgery if persistent diplopia or large defect / muscle entrapment.  Why not the others A Anti-VEGF is intravitreal therapy for retinal disease. C Topical ciprofloxacin drops do not treat a fracture — see red-flag note. D Aciclovir treats HSV keratitis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: DENDRITIC ULCER with terminal end-bulbs is pathognomonic of HERPES SIMPLEX keratitis → topical aciclovir.  Why not the others A Intravitreal therapy is for retinal disease. B Decongestant for nasal/orbital indications. C Bacterial keratitis gives a dense round infiltrate, not a dendrite.

questions.keyPoint: Diabetic macular oedema = leading cause of visual loss in diabetics; anti-VEGF is first-line for centre-involving disease (NICE NG / RCOphth).
questions.keyDifferentiator: Dendritic ulcer with TERMINAL END-BULBS = HSV keratitis → aciclovir; NEVER topical steroids (risk of geographic ulcer / corneal melt).

rows: First-line for centre-involving diabetic macular oedema / wet AMD
Blow-out fracture care; advise no nose-blowing

questions.keyPoint: Blow-out fracture: decongestant + oral antibiotic + DON'T blow the nose. White-eyed blow-out with entrapment in a child is a surgical emergency.

flag.note: The app dropdown displayed 'Ciprofloxacin eye drops' for this scenario. Topical cipro does not manage an orbital floor fracture; the correct option is a NASAL DECONGESTANT (the student's handwritten correction). Standard teaching: decongestant + oral antibiotic prophylaxis + avoid nose-blowing.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old female with SEVERE DYSPHAGIA for 3 days, RIGHT-sided sore throat and high fever. Throat exam: oropharyngeal congestion with the UVULA DEVIATED TO THE LEFT.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Unilateral severe pain/dysphagia with the uvula pushed AWAY from the affected side indicates a peritonsillar collection (quinsy) → needles aspiration/incision + antibiotics.  Why not the others B Follicular tonsillitis is bilateral/symmetrical without uvular deviation.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Symmetrical enlarged tonsils with white follicular exudate = acute (often bacterial, GAS) tonsillitis. Apply Centor/FeverPAIN criteria.  Why not the others A No unilateral bulge or uvular deviation, so not a quinsy.

questions.keyPoint: Quinsy: unilateral, trismus, 'hot-potato' voice, uvula deviated to the contralateral side.
questions.keyDifferentiator: UVULA DEVIATED AWAY from the swollen side + trismus = quinsy; symmetrical enlarged tonsils with white exudate = follicular tonsillitis.

rows: Unilateral, uvula deviated away, trismus → aspirate/drain
Bilateral white exudate; Centor score guides antibiotics

questions.keyDifferentiator: UVULA DEVIATED AWAY from the swollen side + trismus = quinsy; symmetrical enlarged tonsils with white exudate = follicular tonsillitis.

source.paper: Univ. of Kufa · End Block 1

questions.stem: A 28-year-old female: LEFT PAINFUL RED eye + photophobia 4 days AFTER GLAUCOMA SURGERY. Exam: CORNEAL OEDEMA and HYPOPYON.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Pain, reduced vision and HYPOPYON shortly after INTRAOCULAR SURGERY = post-operative endophthalmitis — a sight-threatening emergency (urgent vitreous tap + intravitreal antibiotics).  Why not the others G Uveitis lacks the recent surgical trigger. B AACG: fixed mid-dilated pupil, no hypopyon. D Conjunctivitis spares the cornea/anterior chamber.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Sudden painful red eye + headache/vomiting = AACG (raised IOP). Look for a fixed mid-dilated oval pupil, hazy cornea and haloes around lights.  Why not the others C Open-angle glaucoma is painless and chronic. A No surgical history. G Uveitis has a small pupil, not vomiting.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Bilateral red eye with purulent/mucopurulent discharge and lids stuck in the morning = bacterial conjunctivitis (usually self-limiting; chloramphenicol if needed).  Why not the others E Allergic = itch + watery/stringy discharge. G/B Intra-ocular causes give pain/photophobia, not discharge.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Inflammatory back pain worse in the morning = ANKYLOSING SPONDYLITIS (HLA-B27), classically associated with acute anterior uveitis (may have a hypopyon).  Why not the others A No intraocular surgery. F Scleritis gives a tender nodule, not hypopyon. B AACG: nausea/vomiting, fixed mid-dilated pupil.

questions.keyPoint: Hypopyon + pain + falling vision after intraocular surgery = endophthalmitis until proven otherwise.
questions.keyDifferentiator: HYPOPYON after intraocular surgery = endophthalmitis (A); hypopyon with systemic inflammatory disease (e.g. ankylosing spondylitis) = anterior uveitis (G).

rows: Post-op hypopyon + pain → emergency intravitreal antibiotics
HLA-B27 / AS; small irregular pupil, cells/flare
Painful red eye + vomiting + fixed mid-dilated pupil

questions.stem: A 38-year-old female: painful red eye 10 days, MULTIPLE PREVIOUS EPISODES, a RHEUMATIC disorder, and a TENDER SUB-CONJUNCTIVAL MASS.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Pain, reduced vision and HYPOPYON shortly after INTRAOCULAR SURGERY = post-operative endophthalmitis — a sight-threatening emergency (urgent vitreous tap + intravitreal antibiotics).  Why not the others G Uveitis lacks the recent surgical trigger. B AACG: fixed mid-dilated pupil, no hypopyon. D Conjunctivitis spares the cornea/anterior chamber.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Sudden painful red eye + headache/vomiting = AACG (raised IOP). Look for a fixed mid-dilated oval pupil, hazy cornea and haloes around lights.  Why not the others C Open-angle glaucoma is painless and chronic. A No surgical history. G Uveitis has a small pupil, not vomiting.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Deep boring pain + a TENDER nodule that does not move + systemic rheumatic disease (RA) = nodular scleritis. Scleritis is tender; episcleritis is not.  Why not the others E Allergic conjunctivitis = itch, not a tender nodule. G Uveitis lacks a discrete nodule.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Inflammatory back pain worse in the morning = ANKYLOSING SPONDYLITIS (HLA-B27), classically associated with acute anterior uveitis (may have a hypopyon).  Why not the others A No intraocular surgery. F Scleritis gives a tender nodule, not hypopyon. B AACG: nausea/vomiting, fixed mid-dilated pupil.

questions.keyPoint: Scleritis = TENDER, deep, vision-threatening, vasculitis-associated; episcleritis = non-tender, benign.
questions.keyDifferentiator: HYPOPYON after intraocular surgery = endophthalmitis (A); hypopyon with systemic inflammatory disease (e.g. ankylosing spondylitis) = anterior uveitis (G).

rows: Post-op hypopyon + pain → emergency intravitreal antibiotics

source.paper: Univ. of Kufa · Formative Exam Block 2

questions.stem: A 25-year-old male: nasal obstruction 1 year, POST-NASAL DRIP, FACIAL PRESSURE and LOSS OF SMELL. Endoscopy: NASAL POLYPOSIS.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Itch + sneeze + watery rhinorrhoea + pale/bluish boggy turbinates = allergic rhinitis (IgE-mediated).  Why not the others B Rhinitis medicamentosa needs a decongestant-overuse history. F CRS: facial pressure, polyps.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Unilateral obstruction + bloody discharge + irregular mass in an older adult is a RED-FLAG for sinonasal malignancy (adenocarcinoma classically linked to woodworkers).  Why not the others H Inverted papilloma is benign but locally aggressive — also unilateral; biopsy distinguishes. D Angiofibroma is a young male's vascular nasopharyngeal mass.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Young male + unilateral obstruction + recurrent epistaxis + vascular nasopharyngeal mass = juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma. Do NOT biopsy in clinic (haemorrhage risk); image first.  Why not the others C Malignancy is older with an irregular friable mass. H Inverted papilloma rarely bleeds dramatically.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Symptoms >12 weeks (obstruction, facial pressure, hyposmia, discharge) with polyps on endoscopy = chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps.  Why not the others G Acute rhinosinusitis is <12 weeks, post-viral. A Allergic rhinitis: itch/sneeze, pale turbinates.

questions.keyPoint: CRS diagnosis needs ≥2 symptoms >12 weeks + endoscopic/CT confirmation; anosmia favours polyps.
questions.keyDifferentiator: UNILATERAL obstruction + BLOODY discharge in an OLDER adult = malignancy (C/H); UNILATERAL + EPISTAXIS in a YOUNG male with a vascular mass = angiofibroma (D).

rows: Unilateral + bloody + irregular mass in adult → biopsy
Young male + epistaxis + vascular mass; no clinic biopsy

questions.stem: A 66-year-old female with 4-year AMD presents with SUDDEN MARKED reduction in vision of the left eye — cannot recognise faces or read even with glasses.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Blunt orbital trauma → CT (thin-slice, coronal) to assess orbital floor fracture, herniation and entrapment.  Why not the others B FFA images the retinal circulation, not bony injury.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Sudden deterioration on background dry AMD suggests conversion to WET AMD (choroidal neovascularisation). FFA (with OCT) demonstrates leakage and guides anti-VEGF.  Why not the others A CT is for bony/orbital trauma, not retinal leakage.

questions.keyPoint: New sudden distortion/loss in AMD = wet AMD; image (OCT/FFA) and refer urgently for anti-VEGF.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Sudden vision drop on a background of dry AMD = suspect WET AMD → FFA (± OCT); orbital trauma → CT.

source.paper: Univ. of Kufa · Formative Exam Block 2

questions.stem: A 34-year-old male sustained a BLOW from a TENNIS BALL to the right eye with severe periorbital swelling.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Blunt orbital trauma → CT (thin-slice, coronal) to assess orbital floor fracture, herniation and entrapment.  Why not the others B FFA images the retinal circulation, not bony injury.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Sudden deterioration on background dry AMD suggests conversion to WET AMD (choroidal neovascularisation). FFA (with OCT) demonstrates leakage and guides anti-VEGF.  Why not the others A CT is for bony/orbital trauma, not retinal leakage.

questions.keyPoint: Orbital trauma → CT; MRI is avoided if a metallic foreign body is possible.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Sudden vision drop on a background of dry AMD = suspect WET AMD → FFA (± OCT); orbital trauma → CT.

source.paper: Univ. of Kufa · Formative Exam Block 2

questions.stem: A 12-year-old boy: BILATERAL hearing loss 6 months with POOR SCHOOL PERFORMANCE. TMs are YELLOW and RETRACTED.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Age-related bilateral symmetrical high-frequency SNHL with normal otoscopy = presbycusis.  Why not the others D OME has an abnormal TM. C No vertigo episodes.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The triad of episodic rotational vertigo + fluctuating SNHL + tinnitus, with aural fullness = Meniere (endolymphatic hydrops).  Why not the others B Vestibular neuritis: vertigo without hearing loss. A Presbycusis lacks vertigo.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: 'Glue ear': retracted, dull/yellow TM with conductive loss in a child; commonest cause of childhood hearing loss.  Why not the others A Presbycusis is age-related SNHL. C Meniere is episodic vertigo + SNHL.

questions.keyPoint: Child + poor school performance + retracted dull TM = OME; manage with watchful waiting ± grommets.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Vertigo + tinnitus + fullness + fluctuating SNHL = Meniere; vertigo WITHOUT hearing loss = vestibular neuritis.

rows: Child, retracted dull TM, conductive loss

questions.stem: A 55-year-old DIABETIC with a painful LEFT ear RESISTANT to oral analgesia. Otoscopy: GRANULATION TISSUE in the canal.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Elderly/diabetic + severe pain + granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction = malignant (necrotising) OE — Pseudomonas skull-base osteomyelitis. Needs IV antipseudomonals + imaging.  Why not the others C Otomycosis: itchy, fungal debris, less destructive. A OME is painless effusion.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Herpes zoster oticus: painful vesicles in the concha/EAC + ipsilateral LMN facial palsy = Ramsay Hunt. Treat early with aciclovir + steroid.  Why not the others B No diabetes/granulation. C Fungal otomycosis has no facial palsy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Otalgia with a normal ear exam + a head/neck primary = referred pain (vagus/CN X via Arnold's nerve from larynx/pharynx). Always examine the upper aerodigestive tract.  Why not the others B Malignant OE has abnormal canal findings.

questions.keyPoint: Diabetic + granulation tissue + cranial nerve risk = malignant OE; treat as osteomyelitis.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Diabetic + granulation tissue + pain out of proportion = malignant OE (skull-base osteomyelitis); NORMAL ear exam with a head/neck primary = referred otalgia.

rows: Diabetic, granulation tissue, Pseudomonas, skull base

questions.stem: EMERGENCY INTUBATION 3 months ago, now hoarse 8 weeks with a WHITISH MASS on the POSTERIOR laryngeal cord.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Left recurrent laryngeal nerve has a long intrathoracic course (around the aortic arch) → injured by lung surgery/tumour → paramedian cord = unilateral palsy.  Why not the others A No laryngeal mass described. D Acute laryngitis is transient, infective.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Posterior-cord (vocal process) lesion after intubation = intubation granuloma/granulation tissue.  Why not the others E Nodules are at the anterior-1/3 junction, bilateral, voice abuse. A Ca is older, smoker, progressive.

questions.keyPoint: Posterior cord lesion post-intubation = granuloma; anterior bilateral = nodules.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Post-intubation posterior cord mass = granulation tissue; recurrent laryngeal nerve injury (lung/thyroid surgery) = paramedian cord = vocal cord paralysis.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old DIABETIC male with SUDDEN vision deterioration. Fundoscopy: CHERRY-RED SPOT at the macula.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Sudden painless monocular loss + pale retina with a CHERRY-RED SPOT = central retinal artery occlusion (an ocular 'stroke').  Why not the others D Glaucoma is chronic peripheral loss. A Diabetic maculopathy is gradual.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Young patient, progressive irregular astigmatism (frequent prescription changes) → corneal thinning/cone; advanced disease needs corneal transplant.  Why not the others F Presbyopia is age-related near-blur, not progressive astigmatism. D Glaucoma is field loss.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Progressive peripheral field loss confirmed on perimetry, managed surgically (trabeculectomy) when medical control fails = glaucoma.  Why not the others E A parietal lesion gives a quadrantic homonymous defect, not generalised peripheral constriction. B Not sudden.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Age-related loss of accommodation around 40 → near-vision difficulty with normal distance acuity = presbyopia (reading add).  Why not the others C Keratoconus distorts distance vision too. B Not sudden painless loss.

questions.keyPoint: Cherry-red spot = CRAO; ocular emergency — exclude GCA, treat as a stroke.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Visual-field localisation — INFERIOR homonymous quadrantanopia = PARIETAL lobe; SUPERIOR = TEMPORAL ('PITS').

questions.stem: A 30-year-old LEAD SINGER of a rock band with a 2-month hoarse voice. PAINLESS, no dysphagia.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Repeated intubation traumatises the posterior cord (vocal process) → granuloma.  Why not the others E Palsy gives a paramedian immobile cord, not a mass. B Nodules relate to voice abuse.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Chronic voice overuse → bilateral 'singer's nodules' at the anterior-1/3 junction; painless hoarseness, treat with voice therapy.  Why not the others A Granuloma follows intubation/reflux. D SCC is older smokers, progressive.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Older smoker + a mass arising from the vocal cord = laryngeal SCC (glottic), here with local extension.  Why not the others H Oesophageal/hypopharyngeal Ca starts beyond the larynx. E Palsy is no mass.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Progressive dysphagia + a pyriform fossa mass points to a hypopharyngeal/oesophageal malignancy. Among the listed options, carcinoma oesophagus (H) is the best fit.  Why not the others D Laryngeal SCC arises on the cord, not the pyriform fossa.

questions.keyPoint: Voice abuse + bilateral cord swellings = nodules; first-line is speech therapy.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Voice-overuse bilateral nodules vs post-intubation granuloma vs smoker's laryngeal SCC vs post-thyroidectomy RLN palsy.

rows: Voice abuse, bilateral, painless → speech therapy

choices.explanation: Granuloma following instrumentation/foreign-body removal → reactive pink mass on the cord.  Why not the others H Ca is older/smoker with a friable necrotic mass. D Nodules are bilateral, voice abuse.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Thyroid malignancy invades/compresses the RLN → unilateral paramedian cord palsy.  Why not the others H No laryngeal mass. B Chronic laryngitis is inflammatory, mobile cords.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A pyriform fossa (hypopharynx) mass with dysphagia preceding hoarseness = hypopharyngeal SCC.  Why not the others H Laryngeal Ca starts on the cord with hoarseness first.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: A mass arising from the vocal cord (glottis) with local spread = laryngeal SCC.  Why not the others E Hypopharyngeal Ca arises in the pyriform fossa, not the cord.

questions.keyPoint: Dysphagia first, then hoarseness, with a pyriform mass = hypopharyngeal Ca.
questions.keyDifferentiator: BILATERAL sessile cord swellings = nodules (D) vs UNILATERAL pedunculated = polyp (G); mass AT pyriform fossa = hypopharyngeal (E) vs mass FROM vocal cord = laryngeal (H).

rows: Mass from the cord, hoarseness first

source.paper: Univ. of Kufa · End Block 3

questions.stem: An infant brought for surgery to treat CONGENITAL GLAUCOMA — the eye shows…

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Blood layering in the anterior chamber after blunt trauma = hyphema (rest, head elevation, watch IOP).  Why not the others I Hypopyon is pus, not blood. E Buphthalmos is infantile glaucoma.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Retinal ischaemia drives VEGF → new vessels (proliferative diabetic retinopathy); risk of vitreous haemorrhage → pan-retinal photocoagulation.  Why not the others D Drusen are AMD deposits. A Hyphema is traumatic.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Raised IOP in an infant stretches the elastic globe → buphthalmos ('ox eye') with corneal enlargement/haze, watering and photophobia.  Why not the others A Hyphema follows trauma. D Drusen are age-related macular deposits.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Per the marked key, the answer is a dendritic ulcer (HERPES SIMPLEX keratitis), the classic 'viral' corneal sign.  Why not the others C If the stem means viral CONJUNCTIVITIS, the classic sign is a FOLLICULAR reaction — see flag. H Papillary reaction = bacterial/allergic.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Bacterial conjunctivitis → PAPILLARY reaction (H); viral conjunctivitis → FOLLICULAR reaction (C); HSV keratitis → DENDRITIC ulcer (G).

source.paper: Univ. of Kufa · End Block 3

choices.label: A

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Bacterial (and allergic) conjunctivitis produce a PAPILLARY reaction (cobblestone, with central vessels).  Why not the others C Follicular reaction = viral/chlamydial. I Hypopyon is intraocular pus.

questions.keyPoint: HSV keratitis = dendritic ulcer (fluorescein, terminal bulbs); viral conjunctivitis = follicles.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Bacterial conjunctivitis → PAPILLARY reaction (H); viral conjunctivitis → FOLLICULAR reaction (C); HSV keratitis → DENDRITIC ulcer (G).

flag.note: The key marks G (dendritic ulcer = HSV keratitis). If the intended stem is viral CONJUNCTIVITIS (contrasting with the bacterial/papillary case below), the expected sign is a FOLLICULAR reaction (C). Both are 'viral' — the wording is ambiguous.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with difficulty READING newspapers and close-up work; DISTANCE VISION NORMAL; no eye disease.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reading glasses with a CONVEX (plus) lens
choices.explanation: Presbyopia: age-related loss of accommodation → a converging (plus/convex) reading add focuses near objects on the retina.  Why not the others B A concave lens diverges light — corrects myopia (distance), not reading.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Myopia: the eye is too long/strong, so distant images focus in front of the retina; a diverging (minus/concave) lens corrects distance vision.  Why not the others A A convex lens converges light — used for hypermetropia/presbyopia.

questions.keyPoint: Near-blur + normal distance = presbyopia → convex reading add.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Near-blur with normal distance in an older adult (presbyopia/hypermetropia) → CONVEX; distance-blur in a young person (myopia) → CONCAVE.

source.paper: Univ. of Kufa · Formative Block 3

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reading glasses with a CONVEX (plus) lens
choices.explanation: Presbyopia: age-related loss of accommodation → a converging (plus/convex) reading add focuses near objects on the retina.  Why not the others B A concave lens diverges light — corrects myopia (distance), not reading.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Myopia: the eye is too long/strong, so distant images focus in front of the retina; a diverging (minus/concave) lens corrects distance vision.  Why not the others A A convex lens converges light — used for hypermetropia/presbyopia.

questions.keyPoint: Distance-blur, clear near = myopia → concave (minus) lens.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Near-blur with normal distance in an older adult (presbyopia/hypermetropia) → CONVEX; distance-blur in a young person (myopia) → CONCAVE.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old female: BILATERAL obstruction 4 months; LOCAL VASOCONSTRICTOR spray ~1 year; HYPERTROPHIED inferior turbinates.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Rebound congestion from decongestant overuse. (Full discussion: Formative Exam Block 2, Case 3.)  Why not the others B Allergic = itch/sneeze, pale turbinates.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Itch/sneeze + pale boggy turbinates = allergic rhinitis. (Full discussion: Block 2, Case 5.)  Why not the others A Rhinitis medicamentosa needs decongestant overuse.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Young male + epistaxis + vascular mass = angiofibroma. (Full discussion: Block 2, Case 4.)  Why not the others D CRS has bilateral polyps, not a unilateral vascular mass.

questions.keyPoint: Here the letter is A (was B in Block 2) — letters depend on the local list.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Same scenarios as Block 2 but the OPTION LETTERS differ — always map the answer to THIS theme's list.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old male: persistent nasal obstruction 6 months. Endoscopy: NASAL MUCOSAL OEDEMA with BILATERAL NASAL POLYPS.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Chronic obstruction + bilateral polyps + mucosal oedema = CRS with nasal polyps.  Why not the others C Malignancy is unilateral, friable, with epistaxis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Bilateral boggy compressible septal swelling after trauma = septal haematoma — an EMERGENCY: drain urgently to prevent abscess and cartilage necrosis (saddle-nose deformity).  Why not the others A CRS is gradual, not post-trauma.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Unilateral progressive obstruction + epistaxis + orbital signs (proptosis, diplopia) = sinonasal malignancy with orbital invasion.  Why not the others A CRS does not cause proptosis or unilateral epistaxis.

questions.keyPoint: Bilateral polyps = CRS; unilateral polyp → exclude malignancy.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Post-trauma BOGGY COMPRESSIBLE bilateral septal swelling = septal haematoma (DRAIN URGENTLY); unilateral progressive obstruction + epistaxis + proptosis/diplopia = malignancy.

source.paper: Univ. of Kufa · Formative Block 6

questions.stem: A 4-year-old (Hassan) after NASAL TRAUMA: 3 days later SEVERE BILATERAL obstruction; anterior rhinoscopy shows a BOGGY COMPRESSIBLE swelling of the SEPTUM.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Chronic obstruction + bilateral polyps + mucosal oedema = CRS with nasal polyps.  Why not the others C Malignancy is unilateral, friable, with epistaxis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Bilateral boggy compressible septal swelling after trauma = septal haematoma — an EMERGENCY: drain urgently to prevent abscess and cartilage necrosis (saddle-nose deformity).  Why not the others A CRS is gradual, not post-trauma.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Unilateral progressive obstruction + epistaxis + orbital signs (proptosis, diplopia) = sinonasal malignancy with orbital invasion.  Why not the others A CRS does not cause proptosis or unilateral epistaxis.

questions.keyPoint: Post-trauma bilateral septal swelling = drain now (prevent saddle nose).
questions.keyDifferentiator: Post-trauma BOGGY COMPRESSIBLE bilateral septal swelling = septal haematoma (DRAIN URGENTLY); unilateral progressive obstruction + epistaxis + proptosis/diplopia = malignancy.

flag.note: Do not miss this: an undrained septal haematoma causes septal cartilage AVASCULAR NECROSIS → saddle-nose deformity, and may abscess. It needs urgent incision and drainage + antibiotics.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old rural female: recurrent PROFUSE NON-OFFENSIVE discharge 3 years; otoscopy shows a BIG CENTRAL PERFORATION.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Central perforation with profuse mucoid (non-offensive) discharge = tubotympanic (safe) CSOM.  Why not the others B OME has an intact retracted TM, no discharge.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Adenoidal hypertrophy (snoring) obstructs the Eustachian tube → OME with retracted, dull, yellow TM and conductive loss.  Why not the others A CSOM has a perforation + discharge.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Age-related symmetrical high-frequency SNHL with normal TMs = presbycusis.  Why not the others E Otosclerosis is conductive and younger.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Episodic vertigo + fluctuating SNHL + tinnitus + aural fullness = Meniere's (endolymphatic hydrops).  Why not the others C Presbycusis lacks vertigo.

questions.keyPoint: Central perforation + mucoid non-smelly discharge = safe CSOM; attic/marginal + foul discharge = cholesteatoma (unsafe).
questions.keyDifferentiator: Family history + conductive loss + PARACUSIS WILLISII (hears better in noise) + Schwartze sign = otosclerosis; elderly bilateral SNHL worse in noise = presbycusis.

source.paper: Univ. of Kufa · End Block 6

choices.label: C

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Young adult + family history + conductive loss + paracusis Willisii (hears better in noisy places) = otosclerosis (stapes fixation). A pink-tinged TM = Schwartze sign.  Why not the others C Presbycusis is SNHL, worse in noise. D No vertigo.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Family history + conductive loss + PARACUSIS WILLISII (hears better in noise) + Schwartze sign = otosclerosis; elderly bilateral SNHL worse in noise = presbycusis.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old accountant: SUDDEN PAINFUL vision loss after late office work; UNILATERAL HEADACHE + NAUSEA; CONJUNCTIVAL CONGESTION, CORNEAL OEDEMA, MID-DILATED FIXED PUPIL.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Painful red eye + headache/nausea + hazy cornea + fixed mid-dilated pupil (worse in dim light) = AACG.  Why not the others B/D Retinal vascular occlusions are PAINLESS. C Optic neuritis is subacute, painless on rest.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Sudden painless severe loss + RAPD + pale oedematous retina (cherry-red spot) = CRAO.  Why not the others D CRVO shows haemorrhages, not a pale retina. A AACG is painful.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Subacute monocular loss + dyschromatopsia (red desaturation) + RAPD in a young woman = optic neuritis (consider MS; MRI).  Why not the others B CRAO is sudden and severe with a pale retina. E RD has flashes/floaters.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Widespread flame haemorrhages + dilated tortuous veins ('blood and thunder') in a vasculopath = CRVO.  Why not the others B CRAO gives a pale retina, not haemorrhages.

questions.keyPoint: Painful loss + vomiting + fixed mid-dilated pupil = AACG (emergency).
questions.keyDifferentiator: PALE retina/cherry-red spot + RAPD (painless) = CRAO; FLAME HAEMORRHAGES 'blood & thunder' = CRVO; decreased COLOUR vision + RAPD over days = optic neuritis.

rows: Painful loss, fixed mid-dilated pupil, vomiting
Subacute, decreased colour vision, RAPD, young

source.paper: Univ. of Kufa · End Block 6

questions.stem: A 30-year-old female: SUDDEN PAINLESS loss right eye, marked RAPD; VA hand-movements right / 6-6 left; PALE RETINA on the affected side.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Painful red eye + headache/nausea + hazy cornea + fixed mid-dilated pupil (worse in dim light) = AACG.  Why not the others B/D Retinal vascular occlusions are PAINLESS. C Optic neuritis is subacute, painless on rest.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Sudden painless severe loss + RAPD + pale oedematous retina (cherry-red spot) = CRAO.  Why not the others D CRVO shows haemorrhages, not a pale retina. A AACG is painful.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Subacute monocular loss + dyschromatopsia (red desaturation) + RAPD in a young woman = optic neuritis (consider MS; MRI).  Why not the others B CRAO is sudden and severe with a pale retina. E RD has flashes/floaters.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Widespread flame haemorrhages + dilated tortuous veins ('blood and thunder') in a vasculopath = CRVO.  Why not the others B CRAO gives a pale retina, not haemorrhages.

questions.keyPoint: Pale retina + RAPD + sudden painless loss = CRAO; exclude GCA (ESR/CRP).
questions.keyDifferentiator: PALE retina/cherry-red spot + RAPD (painless) = CRAO; FLAME HAEMORRHAGES 'blood & thunder' = CRVO; decreased COLOUR vision + RAPD over days = optic neuritis.

rows: Painful loss, fixed mid-dilated pupil, vomiting
Subacute, decreased colour vision, RAPD, young

source.paper: تطبيق ENT (app) · ENT App

questions.stem: A CHILD with BLOODY ear discharge and nasal obstruction, after a night of RESTLESSNESS and FEVER.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A febrile, restless child who then develops bloody/purulent otorrhoea = ASOM with spontaneous TM perforation (the discharge relieves the pain).  Why not the others B Hemotympanum is blood behind an INTACT TM (trauma/skull #), with no discharge. D 'Perforated TM' is a sign, not the underlying diagnosis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Foul discharge + vertigo provoked by pressure (positive FISTULA SIGN) = cholesteatoma eroding into the labyrinth — an 'unsafe' ear needing surgery.  Why not the others D A perforation alone doesn't explain the foul smell + vertigo. F Otitis externa is a canal infection without vertigo.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Otorrhoea through a ventilation tube = infected/discharging grommet; treat with topical antibiotic drops.  Why not the others F Otitis externa lacks the recent tube-insertion history.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Discharge with a normal/intact TM points to an EXTERNAL canal infection (otitis externa).  Why not the others A ASOM involves a perforation. C Cholesteatoma is foul attic disease.

questions.keyPoint: Febrile child, then ear discharge = ASOM with perforation.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Foul discharge + vertigo on pressure (fistula sign) = cholesteatoma; clear watery discharge after head trauma = CSF (basal skull fracture).

rows: Febrile child, then discharge via perforation

flag.note: Your in-app selection was Hemotympanum; the model/correct answer is ASOM (febrile child with perforation-related otorrhoea).

questions.stem: A 40-year-old: nasal obstruction with CRUSTS and a BAD ODOUR.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Mucosal atrophy → abnormally wide cavity with foul crusts (ozaena); the patient often cannot smell the odour.  Why not the others B Drug-induced rhinitis needs a systemic-drug history.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Systemic drugs (β-blockers, ACE inhibitors, NSAIDs, COCP) cause nasal congestion = drug-induced rhinitis.  Why not the others G Vasomotor rhinitis is triggered by temperature/irritants, not a drug.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Frontal/ethmoid sinus tenderness after a cold = acute (often bacterial) rhinosinusitis.  Why not the others H A mucocele is a slow expansile cyst, not acute post-cold pain.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Rebound congestion from topical decongestant overuse.  Why not the others B Drug-induced rhinitis is from SYSTEMIC drugs.

questions.keyPoint: Crusts + foul smell + wide nasal cavity = atrophic rhinitis (ozaena).
questions.keyDifferentiator: Systemic drug (β-blocker) trigger = drug-induced rhinitis; topical decongestant overuse = rhinitis medicamentosa; clear discharge worse on bending = CSF rhinorrhoea.

questions.stem: A little boy: TRAUMA while playing, now nasal obstruction with a BILATERAL mass on the nose.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Post-traumatic bilateral septal swelling = septal haematoma — drain urgently to prevent cartilage necrosis (saddle nose).  Why not the others C Angiofibroma is a unilateral vascular nasopharyngeal mass in adolescent males.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Decongestant overuse during a cold → rebound congestion.  Why not the others A Septal haematoma requires trauma.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Adolescent male + obstruction + recurrent epistaxis = juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma; image before any biopsy.  Why not the others B Rhinitis medicamentosa does not bleed.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Black/white fungal spores (Aspergillus niger) filling the canal = otomycosis; aural toilet + antifungal drops.  Why not the others the others are nasal diagnoses — this app item is an ear question within the set.

questions.keyPoint: Bilateral septal mass after trauma = septal haematoma (emergency).
questions.keyDifferentiator: Post-trauma bilateral nasal mass = septal haematoma (drain); black fungal dots in the ear canal = otomycosis.

flag.note: Septal haematoma must be drained urgently — if untreated it progresses to septal abscess and cartilage avascular necrosis (saddle-nose deformity).

questions.stem: (App Q5) Intense ITCHING; otoscopy shows multiple BLACK DOTS in the EAR canal; the TM cannot be visualised.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Post-traumatic bilateral septal swelling = septal haematoma — drain urgently to prevent cartilage necrosis (saddle nose).  Why not the others C Angiofibroma is a unilateral vascular nasopharyngeal mass in adolescent males.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Decongestant overuse during a cold → rebound congestion.  Why not the others A Septal haematoma requires trauma.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Adolescent male + obstruction + recurrent epistaxis = juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma; image before any biopsy.  Why not the others B Rhinitis medicamentosa does not bleed.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Black/white fungal spores (Aspergillus niger) filling the canal = otomycosis; aural toilet + antifungal drops.  Why not the others the others are nasal diagnoses — this app item is an ear question within the set.

questions.keyPoint: Black dots in the ear canal = Aspergillus otomycosis.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Post-trauma bilateral nasal mass = septal haematoma (drain); black fungal dots in the ear canal = otomycosis.

source.paper: تطبيق ENT (app) · ENT App

questions.stem: A 10-year-old: SNORING and progressive hearing loss 4 months; otoscopy shows a YELLOW RETRACTED TM.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Middle-ear fluid WITHOUT acute infection; yellow retracted TM with conductive loss (adenoids → snoring).  Why not the others A AOM is an acutely inflamed, bulging red TM with pain/fever.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Age-related bilateral high-frequency SNHL.  Why not the others E Acoustic neuroma is unilateral.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Per the app key the marked answer is acoustic neuroma. Note, however, that this FLUCTUATING triad is the classic picture of Meniere's disease (see flag).  Why not the others D Autoimmune inner ear disease causes rapidly progressive BILATERAL SNHL.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Foul discharge + attic white keratin debris = chronic otitis media with cholesteatoma (unsafe) → surgery.  Why not the others B OME has no discharge.

questions.keyPoint: Yellow retracted TM + snoring child = OME.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Yellow RETRACTED TM in a snoring child = OME; episodic vertigo + fluctuating SNHL + tinnitus = Meniere's; attic white debris + foul discharge = cholesteatoma.

rows: Episodic vertigo + fluctuating SNHL = Meniere's; progressive unilateral SNHL = neuroma

flag.note: The app marks ACOUSTIC NEUROMA, but INTERMITTENT hearing loss with EPISODIC ROTATIONAL VERTIGO and TINNITUS is the classic triad of MENIERE'S DISEASE. questions.stem: A 22-year-old student: recurrent severe obstruction, SNEEZING and ITCHING, the SAME PERIOD EACH YEAR for 5 years.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Seasonal, itch + sneeze = allergic rhinitis (IgE-mediated).  Why not the others B Vasomotor rhinitis lacks itch and seasonality.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Non-allergic autonomic rhinitis triggered by temperature change, irritants and alcohol.  Why not the others C NARES shows nasal eosinophilia without a temperature trigger.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Pregnancy/hormonal rhinitis; typically resolves after delivery.  Why not the others A Allergic rhinitis is itch/seasonal.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Symptoms tied to the workplace (flour) with relief away from work = occupational rhinitis.  Why not the others A Allergic rhinitis is not specifically work-related.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Seasonal itch/sneeze = allergic; temperature/irritant triggers (no allergy) = vasomotor; pregnancy = hormonal; work-related, better on holiday = occupational.

rows: Worse at work, better on holiday

questions.stem: An 18-MONTH-OLD boy: 3-week history of BLOOD-STAINED, FOUL-SMELLING discharge from the LEFT nostril. No trauma. Left nasal canal inflamed; opposite side normal.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Cheek tenderness + pain worse on bending/coughing after a cold = acute maxillary sinusitis (the commonest sinus affected).  Why not the others B Frontal sinusitis = forehead pain/tenderness. A Ethmoid = pain between the eyes.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Seasonal, outdoor-triggered sneezing + watery rhinorrhoea + eye irritation = allergic rhinitis.  Why not the others J Non-allergic rhinitis lacks the allergen/seasonal pattern and eye symptoms. F Chronic sinusitis has facial pressure/polyps.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A septal perforation + recurrent epistaxis in a homeless adult points to chemical irritation (classically intranasal COCAINE) damaging the septal mucosa/cartilage.  Why not the others L Wegener's causes crusting + saddle nose + systemic features (renal/lung). K Spontaneous epistaxis has no septal defect.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: UNILATERAL foul-smelling (± blood-stained) nasal discharge in a young child = a retained nasal FOREIGN BODY until proven otherwise.  Why not the others D Allergic rhinitis is bilateral, itch/sneeze, not foul/unilateral. F Chronic sinusitis is uncommon at this age and bilateral.

questions.keyPoint: Unilateral foul nasal discharge in a child = foreign body — examine and remove.
questions.keyDifferentiator: UNILATERAL FOUL discharge in a toddler = nasal foreign body; cheek tenderness/pain worse on bending after a cold = maxillary sinusitis; homeless + septal perforation + epistaxis = chemical (cocaine) irritation.

rows: Unilateral foul discharge in a child

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman: 6-month worsening deafness with RINGING (tinnitus) and OCCASIONAL DIZZY SPELLS where the ROOM SPINS.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Progressive unilateral SNHL (Weber to the better/right ear) + tinnitus + facial-nerve involvement + headache = a cerebellopontine-angle tumour (vestibular schwannoma / acoustic neuroma) → MRI IAM.  Why not the others F Ménière is episodic with no facial-nerve signs. H Otosclerosis is conductive, no CN signs.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Child + recent URTI + bulging red TM + ear tugging = acute (suppurative) otitis media.  Why not the others G Otitis externa: canal swelling/discharge, painful tragus, normal TM. D Chronic effusion is painless, dull retracted TM.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Fluctuating SNHL + tinnitus + episodic rotational vertigo = Ménière's (endolymphatic hydrops).  Why not the others A Acoustic neuroma = progressive unilateral SNHL, not episodic vertigo. H Otosclerosis is conductive, no vertigo.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Young woman + conductive (Rinne-negative) low-frequency loss + family history = otosclerosis (stapes fixation). Audiogram may show Carhart's notch.  Why not the others F Ménière has vertigo + SNHL. A Acoustic neuroma is SNHL, unilateral, with CN signs.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Conductive loss + low-frequency + family hx + Rinne negative = otosclerosis; unilateral SNHL + tinnitus + facial-nerve signs = acoustic neuroma; attic perforation + foul flaky discharge = cholesteatoma.

rows: Conductive, low-frequency, family hx, Carhart's notch
Unilateral SNHL + tinnitus + CN signs → MRI

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy: 2-day RIGHT ear pain with a RUNNY NOSE, constantly TUGGING at his ear; INFLAMED, BULGING tympanic membrane.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Progressive unilateral SNHL (Weber to the better/right ear) + tinnitus + facial-nerve involvement + headache = a cerebellopontine-angle tumour (vestibular schwannoma / acoustic neuroma) → MRI IAM.  Why not the others F Ménière is episodic with no facial-nerve signs. H Otosclerosis is conductive, no CN signs.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Child + recent URTI + bulging red TM + ear tugging = acute (suppurative) otitis media.  Why not the others G Otitis externa: canal swelling/discharge, painful tragus, normal TM. D Chronic effusion is painless, dull retracted TM.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Attic (superior/marginal) perforation + foul flaky keratin discharge = cholesteatoma — an 'unsafe' ear needing surgery.  Why not the others E CSOM (safe) has a CENTRAL perforation + mucoid non-offensive discharge. G Otitis externa is a canal infection.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Fluctuating SNHL + tinnitus + episodic rotational vertigo = Ménière's (endolymphatic hydrops).  Why not the others A Acoustic neuroma = progressive unilateral SNHL, not episodic vertigo. H Otosclerosis is conductive, no vertigo.

questions.keyPoint: Child + URTI + bulging red TM = AOM/ASOM.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Conductive loss + low-frequency + family hx + Rinne negative = otosclerosis; unilateral SNHL + tinnitus + facial-nerve signs = acoustic neuroma; attic perforation + foul flaky discharge = cholesteatoma.

rows: Conductive, low-frequency, family hx, Carhart's notch

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman: 6-month history of intermittent RINGING (tinnitus) and dizziness, with GRADUAL worsening of hearing in her RIGHT ear; exam unremarkable.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Brief vertigo triggered by head position with NORMAL hearing = BPPV (otoconia in the posterior semicircular canal); Dix–Hallpike positive, treat with Epley manoeuvre.  Why not the others E Ménière has hearing loss/tinnitus. D Labyrinthitis is constant, not positional.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Aminoglycosides (e.g. gentamicin for pyelonephritis) are cochleo- and vestibulotoxic → bilateral SNHL + imbalance.  Why not the others E Ménière is unilateral and episodic. A BPPV has no hearing loss.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Per the key the answer is labyrinthitis. Strictly, acute isolated vertigo with NO hearing loss is VESTIBULAR NEURITIS; labyrinthitis adds cochlear involvement (hearing loss). As the list has no 'vestibular neuritis', labyrinthitis is the closest option.  Why not the others A BPPV is positional and brief. H Posterior-circulation stroke usually has other brainstem/cerebellar signs (HINTS exam).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Tinnitus + (fluctuating) hearing loss + episodic vertigo = Ménière's disease.  Why not the others A BPPV has no hearing loss/tinnitus. H Posterior-circulation stroke is sudden with other neuro signs.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Brief positional vertigo with NO hearing loss = BPPV; vertigo + hearing loss + tinnitus = Ménière; aminoglycoside + bilateral SNHL = ototoxicity; ear rash + facial palsy = Ramsay Hunt.

rows: Positional brief vertigo, normal hearing → Epley

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman: LEFT-sided FACIAL PALSY and VERTIGO, impaired hearing on the LEFT, and a VESICULAR RASH around the EXTERNAL AUDITORY MEATUS.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Brief vertigo provoked by head-position change with normal hearing = benign (paroxysmal) positional vertigo; Dix–Hallpike positive, treat with Epley.  Why not the others F Ménière has hearing loss/tinnitus. I Vestibular neuronitis is continuous, not positional.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Recurrent episodic vertigo (minutes–hours) + fluctuating SNHL + tinnitus ± aural fullness = Ménière's.  Why not the others B BPV is brief, positional, with NORMAL hearing. I Vestibular neuronitis is a single prolonged episode without hearing loss.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Acute prolonged vertigo following a viral illness, WITHOUT hearing loss = vestibular neuronitis (labyrinthitis would add hearing loss).  Why not the others F Ménière is recurrent with hearing loss. B BPV is positional and brief.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Herpes zoster oticus: vesicular rash in the EAM/concha + ipsilateral LMN facial palsy + vestibulocochlear involvement (vertigo, SNHL) = Ramsay Hunt.  Why not the others I Vestibular neuronitis has NO rash, NO facial palsy and NO hearing loss. F Ménière has no rash/facial palsy.

questions.keyPoint: Vesicular ear rash + facial palsy = Ramsay Hunt (early aciclovir + steroid).
questions.keyDifferentiator: Ear rash + facial palsy = Ramsay Hunt (J); episodic vertigo + hearing loss + tinnitus = Ménière (F); crossed brainstem signs + Horner's + dysphagia = lateral medullary syndrome (D); positional, normal hearing = BPV (B); post-viral vertigo, no hearing loss = vestibular neuronitis (I). NB the list prints C 'vestibular schwannoma' AND H 'acoustic neuroma' — these are synonyms (neither is the keyed answer here).

flag.note: Your handwritten answer was I (vestibular neuronitis); the correct answer is J (Ramsay Hunt syndrome) — a vesicular rash around the EAM with facial palsy + ipsilateral hearing loss is diagnostic. (Also, 'each option used once': vestibular neuronitis = I is needed for Case 5.)

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman WAKES at night with a SPINNING sensation + nausea lasting about 30 SECONDS, most obvious turning onto the RIGHT side.

choices.label: A

choices.label: D

choices.label: B

choices.label: D

choices.label: H

questions.stem: A 40-year-old ASTHMATIC: gradual blockage of BOTH nostrils, PALE SWELLINGS in both cavities; sensitive to ASPIRIN.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Unilateral foul nasal discharge in a child = retained nasal foreign body.  Why not the others B Allergic rhinitis is bilateral and itchy.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Year-round symptoms worse at night = perennial allergic rhinitis (house-dust mite).  Why not the others C Rhinitis medicamentosa needs decongestant overuse.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Post-traumatic UNILATERAL fixed obstruction = deviated nasal septum.  Why not the others F Deviated nasal bones cause external deformity rather than isolated unilateral blockage.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Samter's triad (asthma + aspirin sensitivity + nasal polyps); pale grey polyps cause bilateral obstruction/anosmia.  Why not the others B Allergic rhinitis gives boggy turbinates, not discrete pale polyps.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old LEAD SINGER of a rock band: 2-month PAINLESS hoarse voice, no dysphagia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Voice overuse → bilateral singer's nodules; treat with speech therapy.  Why not the others D SCC is an older smoker with progressive symptoms.

choices.label: D

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury during thyroid surgery → cord palsy.  Why not the others A Granuloma needs intubation/reflux.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Singer = nodules; intubation = granuloma; smoker + persistent = laryngeal SCC; post-thyroid surgery = cord palsy; reflux/antacid use = chronic laryngitis.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man: dysphagia + REGURGITATION of UNDIGESTED food after about 5 minutes.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Per the key, a foreign body (e.g. a bone) causing acute odynophagia/difficulty with saliva fits best among the options.  Why not the others B A pouch causes regurgitation of food, not sudden saliva difficulty.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Zenker's diverticulum: regurgitation of undigested food, halitosis, gurgling neck; barium swallow diagnostic.  Why not the others D Carcinoma is progressive with weight loss, not undigested-food regurgitation.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Progressive dysphagia to solids + weight loss + pain = malignancy (hypopharyngeal/oesophageal).  Why not the others F Globus has no true dysphagia/weight loss.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: A lower-neck mass that moves on swallowing = thyroid; a large goitre can cause compressive dysphagia.  Why not the others B A pharyngeal pouch regurgitates undigested food.

questions.keyPoint: Regurgitation of undigested food = pharyngeal pouch.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Regurgitation of UNDIGESTED food = pharyngeal pouch; PROGRESSIVE dysphagia to solids + WEIGHT LOSS = carcinoma; lump that IMPROVES on swallowing = globus; neck swelling MOVING on swallowing = goitre.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man: 2-day severe RIGHT earache after a recent HOLIDAY ABROAD; marked TRAGAL tenderness; canal SWOLLEN and full of DEBRIS.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: AOM with TM perforation → discharge relieves the pain.  Why not the others D Otitis externa has canal/tragal signs, not pain relieved by discharge.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Swimmer's ear: tragal tenderness + canal oedema/debris = acute otitis externa; aural toilet + topical antibiotic/steroid drops.  Why not the others A ASOM affects the middle ear with a bulging TM.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Pre-auricular pain + teeth-grinding (bruxism) with a normal ear = TMJ dysfunction (referred otalgia).  Why not the others D Otitis externa has canal findings.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Diabetic + severe persistent night pain → malignant (necrotising) otitis externa (Pseudomonas skull-base osteomyelitis).  Why not the others D Simple otitis externa is shorter and less severe.

questions.keyPoint: Tragal tenderness + canal debris = otitis externa.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Swimmer + TRAGAL tenderness + canal debris = otitis externa; child URTI then discharge relieves pain = AOM; PINNA VESICLES + facial palsy = herpes zoster oticus; PRE-AURICULAR pain + bruxism = TMJ; DIABETIC + severe persistent night pain = malignant OE.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy: SUDDEN noisy breathing + DROOLING, preceding URTI + pyrexia; appears FRIGHTENED and FLUSHED with inspiratory + expiratory STRIDOR and intercostal in-drawing.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Transient choking/cyanosis then well = inhaled foreign body; risk of later obstruction/infection → bronchoscopy if suspected.  Why not the others E Laryngomalacia is chronic infantile stridor, not a sudden choking episode.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Barking ('seal') cough + stridor, no drooling = croup (parainfluenza); oral dexamethasone.  Why not the others G Epiglottitis has drooling and toxaemia.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Infant inspiratory stridor worse with feeding/exertion ± failure to thrive = laryngomalacia (usually self-resolving).  Why not the others B A foreign body is a sudden choking event, not gradual feeding stridor.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Toxic, drooling, frightened child (tripod) with stridor = acute epiglottitis (H. influenzae b) — do NOT examine the throat; secure the airway.  Why not the others D Croup has a barking cough and NO drooling/toxicity.

questions.stem: Most reliable hearing test for a 3-MONTH-OLD infant.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Dix–Hallpike provokes the characteristic nystagmus/vertigo of BPPV (diagnostic).  Why not the others D Epley is the treatment, not the diagnostic test.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Objective ABR/evoked response audiometry needs no patient cooperation → most reliable in young infants.  Why not the others B/J Distraction & visual reinforcement are behavioural, unreliable <6 months.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Speech audiometry (discrimination scores) complements PTA when fitting hearing aids.  Why not the others G PTA alone gives thresholds but not speech discrimination.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: The Stenger test detects non-organic (feigned) unilateral hearing loss.  Why not the others E Evoked response audiometry is also objective, but the classic malingerer test is the Stenger.

questions.keyPoint: Infant objective hearing test = evoked response audiometry (ABR).
questions.keyDifferentiator: Objective evoked response audiometry for infants; impedance/tympanometry for effusion; Stenger for malingerers; Dix–Hallpike diagnoses BPPV (Epley treats it).

questions.stem: Sensorineural hearing loss of LOW-FREQUENCY sounds.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Aminoglycosides are cochleo- and vestibulotoxic → high-freq SNHL + oscillopsia (bilateral vestibular loss).  Why not the others D Ménière is low-frequency and episodic.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A flat type-B trace indicates middle-ear fluid = glue ear (OME).  Why not the others H Otosclerosis gives a type As / shallow trace, not flat type B.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Ménière classically gives low-frequency SNHL early.  Why not the others I Presbycusis is high-frequency.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Noise damage produces the classic 4 kHz notch.  Why not the others I Presbycusis slopes off at the highest frequencies.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Low-frequency SNHL = Ménière; 3–6 kHz notch = noise-induced; high-freq SNHL + oscillopsia = gentamicin; conductive + Carhart's notch = otosclerosis; type B tympanogram = glue ear.

questions.stem: A 43-year-old woman: episodes lasting ~3 HOURS of spinning + vomiting, RINGING in both ears; IBS history; PTA shows SNHL of LOWER frequencies.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Progressive UNILATERAL SNHL (Weber to the better/left ear) + tinnitus = acoustic neuroma → MRI IAM.  Why not the others F Ménière is episodic and bilateralisable.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Positional brief vertigo (often post-trauma) with normal hearing = BPPV.  Why not the others J Vestibular neuritis is continuous, not positional.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Wallenberg syndrome: vertigo + dysarthria + ipsilateral Horner's + crossed spinothalamic loss (PICA/vertebral).  Why not the others J Vestibular neuritis has no brainstem signs.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Episodic vertigo (hours) + tinnitus + low-frequency SNHL = Ménière.  Why not the others B BPPV is seconds and has no hearing loss.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Hours + tinnitus + low-freq SNHL = Ménière; brainstem signs (Horner's, dysarthria, crossed sensory loss) = lateral medullary; positional + post-trauma, no hearing loss = BPPV; post-viral, no hearing loss = vestibular neuritis (labyrinthitis would have hearing loss).

questions.stem: A 37-year-old man: 3-day vertigo + vomiting, RAPID onset worse on day 1, thought he 'caught a bug'; NO tinnitus/hearing loss; smokes 30/day; bilateral nystagmus.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Positional brief vertigo (often post-trauma) with normal hearing = BPPV.  Why not the others J Vestibular neuritis is continuous, not positional.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Wallenberg syndrome: vertigo + dysarthria + ipsilateral Horner's + crossed spinothalamic loss (PICA/vertebral).  Why not the others J Vestibular neuritis has no brainstem signs.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Episodic vertigo (hours) + tinnitus + low-frequency SNHL = Ménière.  Why not the others B BPPV is seconds and has no hearing loss.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Acute prolonged post-viral vertigo with NO hearing loss = vestibular neuritis.  Why not the others D Labyrinthitis would include hearing loss.

questions.keyPoint: Post-viral vertigo, no hearing loss = vestibular neuritis.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Hours + tinnitus + low-freq SNHL = Ménière; brainstem signs (Horner's, dysarthria, crossed sensory loss) = lateral medullary; positional + post-trauma, no hearing loss = BPPV; post-viral, no hearing loss = vestibular neuritis (labyrinthitis would have hearing loss).

flag.note: Correct = vestibular neuritis (J): post-viral vertigo with NO hearing loss. Labyrinthitis (D) would add hearing loss — don't confuse the two (the handwritten mark is ambiguous between D and J).

source.paper: Master Pass ENT · Master Pass ENT

questions.stem: A 15-year-old girl: right earache since yesterday, NO discharge/fever, TONSILLECTOMY 5 days ago; ear canal and TM NORMAL.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Swimmer + canal oedema/pus = acute otitis externa.  Why not the others F Necrotising OE is in diabetics/immunocompromised with granulation tissue.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Per the key the answer is drug allergy — a contact reaction (e.g. to neomycin in the topical drops) causing peri-auricular dermatitis.  Why not the others F Necrotising OE is in diabetics with granulation tissue, not a young healthy adult.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Post-tonsillectomy otalgia with a normal ear = referred pain (glossopharyngeal nerve).  Why not the others A Otitis externa would show canal signs.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Pre-auricular pain on jaw movement + masticatory tenderness with a normal ear = TMJ dysfunction.  Why not the others H Referred pain has a specific primary source elsewhere.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Earache + NORMAL ear after tonsillectomy = referred pain; pre-auricular pain + masticatory tenderness = TMJ; unilateral tonsil + ulcer = oropharyngeal neoplasia; swimmer + canal pus = otitis externa.

source.paper: Master Pass ENT · Master Pass ENT

choices.label: A

questions.keyPoint: Pinna reaction after topical drops = contact (drug) allergy; but consider perichondritis if febrile/infective.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Earache + NORMAL ear after tonsillectomy = referred pain; pre-auricular pain + masticatory tenderness = TMJ; unilateral tonsil + ulcer = oropharyngeal neoplasia; swimmer + canal pus = otitis externa.

flag.note: Marked answer is drug allergy (contact dermatitis to ear drops). Note a RED, SHINY, TENDER pinna with fever can also represent PERICHONDRITIS / pinna cellulitis (infective) — worth distinguishing clinically.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man: 3–4-month progressive dysphagia + hoarse voice, 8 kg WEIGHT LOSS; COELIAC disease; 35 units/week alcohol; smokes 30/day.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Dysphagia to solids and liquids + bird-beak tapering on barium = achalasia (failure of LOS relaxation).  Why not the others B A benign stricture gives solids-first dysphagia, not bird-beak.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Lump sensation, worse on dry/saliva swallows, no true dysphagia = globus.  Why not the others F Carcinoma causes progressive dysphagia + weight loss.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Progressive dysphagia + weight loss + smoking/alcohol (± coeliac risk) = oesophageal carcinoma.  Why not the others D Globus has no weight loss / true dysphagia.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Undigested-food regurgitation + halitosis = pharyngeal pouch (Zenker's).  Why not the others F Carcinoma is progressive with weight loss.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Progressive solids dysphagia + weight loss = carcinoma; nasal regurgitation + wasted/fasciculating tongue = bulbar palsy; undigested-food regurgitation + halitosis = pharyngeal pouch; lump improving on swallowing = globus; bird-beak on barium = achalasia.

source.paper: Master Pass ENT · Master Pass ENT

questions.stem: Rinne POSITIVE on the left, NEGATIVE on the right; Weber louder on the RIGHT.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Bilateral Rinne negative + central Weber = bilateral conductive loss.  Why not the others B Bilateral SNHL is Rinne positive bilaterally.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A 'negative' Rinne on the left BUT Weber lateralising AWAY (to the right) is a FALSE-NEGATIVE Rinne — the left cochlea is dead, so left bone conduction is heard by the right ear. This is severe LEFT SNHL.  Why not the others C True left conductive loss would lateralise Weber to the LEFT, not the right.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Right Rinne negative (BC>AC) + Weber to the right = right conductive loss.  Why not the others H SNHL would give Weber to the better ear, not the affected one.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Rinne positive both (no conductive component) + Weber to the LEFT (better ear) = right SNHL.  Why not the others D Left SNHL would lateralise Weber to the right.

questions.keyPoint: Rinne− + Weber to same side = conductive on that side.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Weber lateralises TO the affected ear in CONDUCTIVE loss, and AWAY (to the better ear) in SNHL. A Rinne-negative ear whose Weber lateralises AWAY = FALSE-NEGATIVE Rinne (severe SNHL / 'dead ear').

rows: Rinne−; Weber to the affected ear
Rinne+; Weber to the better ear

source.paper: Master Pass ENT · Master Pass ENT

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Symmetrical bilateral SNHL gives Rinne positive both + central Weber.  Why not the others F Normal hearing gives the IDENTICAL pattern — see flag.

choices.label: D

rows: Rinne−; Weber to the affected ear

flag.note: This fork pattern (Rinne+ bilaterally, Weber central) is IDENTICAL for symmetrical bilateral SNHL (B) and NORMAL hearing (F). Tuning forks cannot distinguish them — pure tone audiometry is needed.

questions.topic: Q7 — Management of Inner Ear Disorders
questions.optionsTitle: Q7 — Management of Inner Ear Disorders

source.paper: Master Pass ENT · Master Pass ENT

questions.stem: A 45-year-old Jehovah's Witness: Ménière symptoms 3 years, now CONSTANT with bilateral SNHL + residual unsteadiness; declines invasive procedures.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: BPPV → the Epley repositioning manoeuvre is the treatment.  Why not the others B Dix–Hallpike is diagnostic, not the treatment.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Oral aciclovir, corticosteroids and eye care
choices.explanation: Ramsay Hunt (herpes zoster oticus): antiviral + corticosteroid + EYE CARE (the eye can't close).  Why not the others D Aciclovir alone omits the steroid + eye protection needed here.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Burnt-out bilateral Ménière with residual imbalance, declining invasive options → vestibular rehabilitation + counselling.  Why not the others F Plasmapheresis (a blood product issue for a Jehovah's Witness) is for autoimmune inner-ear disease, not this.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Apparent hearing loss with a normal objective PTA suggests a NON-ORGANIC loss → the Stenger test confirms it.  Why not the others J Telling the patient their hearing is fine is premature without confirming with the Stenger test.

choices.label: J
choices.text: Tell the patient that his/her hearing is better than described

questions.keyPoint: Burnt-out Ménière, declines surgery → reassurance + vestibular rehab.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Burnt-out Ménière declining invasive care = reassurance/rehab; Ramsay Hunt = aciclovir + steroid + eye care; BPPV = Epley; resolving neuritis = reassurance; non-organic loss (normal PTA) = Stenger test.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old girl: 1-year deteriorating school performance, poor on a hearing test, but pure tone audiogram is NORMAL (out of keeping with the apparent loss). What next?

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: BPPV → the Epley repositioning manoeuvre is the treatment.  Why not the others B Dix–Hallpike is diagnostic, not the treatment.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Oral aciclovir, corticosteroids and eye care
choices.explanation: Ramsay Hunt (herpes zoster oticus): antiviral + corticosteroid + EYE CARE (the eye can't close).  Why not the others D Aciclovir alone omits the steroid + eye protection needed here.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Self-limiting (resolving) vestibular neuritis in a patient declining medication → reassurance.  Why not the others G Prochlorperazine helps acute symptoms but she prefers no medication.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Apparent hearing loss with a normal objective PTA suggests a NON-ORGANIC loss → the Stenger test confirms it.  Why not the others J Telling the patient their hearing is fine is premature without confirming with the Stenger test.

choices.label: J
choices.text: Tell the patient that his/her hearing is better than described

questions.stem: A 24-year-old lady: 3-day severe LEFT ear pain (similar episode a month ago resolved with antibiotics), reduced hearing + CONDUCTIVE deafness, NO pinna tenderness; TM red, inflamed, swollen.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Post-auricular swelling pushing the pinna down/forward + persistent fever after AOM = acute mastoiditis (admit, IV antibiotics ± surgery).  Why not the others C AOM alone doesn't displace the pinna.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Swimmer + tragal/pinna tenderness + canal debris with an intact TM = acute otitis externa.  Why not the others C AOM affects the middle ear with a bulging TM.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Red, bulging, inflamed TM with conductive loss = acute otitis media.  Why not the others B Otitis externa has canal/pinna tenderness with a normal TM.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Otogenic intracranial spread with a FOCAL deficit + papilloedema (raised ICP) + meningism = brain abscess.  Why not the others H Meningitis gives meningism but not a focal deficit with papilloedema/mass effect.

questions.keyPoint: Red bulging TM + conductive loss = AOM.

questions.optionsTitle: Q9 — Management of Middle Ear Disorders

source.paper: Master Pass ENT · Master Pass ENT

questions.stem: A 24-year-old lady: 9-month worsening hearing loss, MOTHER wore hearing aids from 38; Rinne NEGATIVE bilaterally, NORMAL TM; wants an alternative to hearing aids.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Attic perforation + foul flaky keratin = cholesteatoma → surgical mastoidectomy.  Why not the others J Topical drops don't eradicate cholesteatoma.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: AOM failing oral antibiotics with developing mastoid tenderness → myringotomy to drain/culture (relieving the middle ear).  Why not the others F Oral antibiotics have already failed.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: AOM in a young child with high fever / systemic upset → oral antibiotics.  Why not the others J Topical drops don't treat middle-ear infection behind an intact TM.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Otosclerosis (family hx, conductive loss, normal TM) → stapedectomy as a surgical alternative to a hearing aid.  Why not the others A Cochlear implants are for profound SNHL, not conductive otosclerosis.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Otosclerosis wanting an alternative to a hearing aid = stapedectomy; child AOM + high fever = oral antibiotics; barotrauma/traumatic perforation = surveillance (most heal); cholesteatoma = mastoidectomy.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old DIABETIC (BM 21.4): severe right ear pain, NO discharge, ERYTHEMA + GRANULATIONS on the canal floor, RIGHT facial palsy (no forehead sparing) + difficulty swallowing.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: A localised tender swelling/boil in the outer cartilaginous canal = furunculosis (Staph).  Why not the others I Necrotising OE is in diabetics with granulations + cranial-nerve signs.

choices.label: H
choices.text: Malignancy of the external auditory canal (EAC)
choices.explanation: Chronic progressive pain + polypoid friable mass + blood + regional nodes + facial-nerve involvement = EAC malignancy (usually SCC).  Why not the others I Necrotising OE has granulations rather than a polypoid bleeding mass + nodes.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Diabetic + granulation tissue + cranial-nerve palsies = necrotising (malignant) otitis externa — Pseudomonas skull-base osteomyelitis.  Why not the others G Furunculosis is a localised canal boil without cranial-nerve signs.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Herpes zoster oticus: concha/EAC vesicles + facial palsy ± vertigo/SNHL.  Why not the others C Bell's palsy has no rash.

questions.keyPoint: Diabetic + granulations + CN palsy = necrotising OE.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old news presenter: hoarseness, no dysphagia, SMOKER (20 pack-year); cords FLUID-FILLED BILATERALLY with polypoid changes.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Muscle-tension (functional) dysphonia: normal cords, stress-related, relieved by laryngeal manipulation → voice therapy/counselling.  Why not the others J Cancer would show a cord lesion.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: A posterior-cord (vocal process) granuloma after prolonged intubation is associated with arytenoid PERICHONDRITIS.  Why not the others J No irregular tumour described.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Smoking-related bilateral fluid accumulation in Reinke's space = Reinke's oedema (low-pitched husky voice).  Why not the others K Nodules are discrete bilateral swellings from voice abuse, not diffuse fluid.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: The non-moving (right) cord identifies a RIGHT recurrent laryngeal nerve injury.  Why not the others E The left cord is moving normally, so the left RLN is intact.

questions.keyPoint: Smoker + bilateral fluid-filled cords = Reinke's oedema.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Smoker + BILATERAL fluid-filled cords = Reinke's oedema; voice overuse + smooth cord mass = nodule; immobile cord after thyroid surgery = recurrent laryngeal nerve (name the SIDE of the still cord); functional/muscle-tension dysphonia (normal cords) = counselling.

questions.stem: A man after HEMI-THYROIDECTOMY: coarser, tiring voice; the LEFT cord adducts but the RIGHT remains in place and 'bows out' on coughing. Which nerve was damaged?

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Muscle-tension (functional) dysphonia: normal cords, stress-related, relieved by laryngeal manipulation → voice therapy/counselling.  Why not the others J Cancer would show a cord lesion.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: A posterior-cord (vocal process) granuloma after prolonged intubation is associated with arytenoid PERICHONDRITIS.  Why not the others J No irregular tumour described.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: The non-moving (right) cord identifies a RIGHT recurrent laryngeal nerve injury.  Why not the others E The left cord is moving normally, so the left RLN is intact.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Voice overuse → a benign vocal cord nodule; manage with voice therapy.  Why not the others J Cancer is usually in older smokers with an irregular/ulcerated lesion.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Smoker + BILATERAL fluid-filled cords = Reinke's oedema; voice overuse + smooth cord mass = nodule; immobile cord after thyroid surgery = recurrent laryngeal nerve (name the SIDE of the still cord); functional/muscle-tension dysphonia (normal cords) = counselling.

source.paper: Master Pass ENT · Master Pass ENT

questions.stem: A young banker: 'strained voice', neck pain + throat lump, NO neck lump but tender; cords NORMAL on scoping; a gentle downward pull of the larynx RELIEVES the pain; busy lifestyle.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Muscle-tension (functional) dysphonia: normal cords, stress-related, relieved by laryngeal manipulation → voice therapy/counselling.  Why not the others J Cancer would show a cord lesion.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: A posterior-cord (vocal process) granuloma after prolonged intubation is associated with arytenoid PERICHONDRITIS.  Why not the others J No irregular tumour described.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Smoking-related bilateral fluid accumulation in Reinke's space = Reinke's oedema (low-pitched husky voice).  Why not the others K Nodules are discrete bilateral swellings from voice abuse, not diffuse fluid.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Voice overuse → a benign vocal cord nodule; manage with voice therapy.  Why not the others J Cancer is usually in older smokers with an irregular/ulcerated lesion.

questions.keyPoint: Normal cords + stress + manipulation relief = muscle-tension dysphonia → counselling.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Smoker + BILATERAL fluid-filled cords = Reinke's oedema; voice overuse + smooth cord mass = nodule; immobile cord after thyroid surgery = recurrent laryngeal nerve (name the SIDE of the still cord); functional/muscle-tension dysphonia (normal cords) = counselling.

questions.stem: Martha: a 'cold that won't go away' 3 weeks, PAINFUL CHEEKS worse on LEANING FORWARD, infraorbital tenderness, clear discharge, no asthma/allergy.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cheek pain worse on bending + infraorbital tenderness over ~3 weeks = acute (maxillary) rhinosinusitis.  Why not the others D Chronic rhinosinusitis is >12 weeks.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Samter's triad = nasal polyps + asthma + aspirin (NSAID) sensitivity.  Why not the others B Allergic rhinitis isn't the 'unique' triad feature being asked for.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Symptoms >12 weeks + anosmia + bilateral disease (and CF predisposition) = chronic rhinosinusitis.  Why not the others A Acute rhinosinusitis is <12 weeks.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Atrophic rhinitis (ozaena) is classically associated with Klebsiella ozenae.  Why not the others G Pseudomonas/Staph are not the classic ozaena organism.

choices.label: I
choices.text: Urgent CT scan, IV antibiotics and drainage

choices.label: J
choices.text: Urgent IV antibiotics, steroids and CT scan

questions.keyPoint: Cheek pain worse leaning forward = acute maxillary sinusitis.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Cheek pain worse leaning forward + infraorbital tenderness <12 wk = acute rhinosinusitis; CF + >12 wk + anosmia = chronic; orbital signs (proptosis/chemosis/colour-vision loss) = orbital cellulitis → urgent CT + IV abx + drainage; polyps + late-onset asthma = aspirin sensitivity; foul crusting ozaena organism = Klebsiella ozenae.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old (Kofi): sore throat, 'strained' breathing, DROOLING, LEANING FORWARD, mouth open, inspiratory STRIDOR, pale/shocked. Next step?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Amoxicillin in glandular fever (EBV) precipitates a maculopapular rash.  Why not the others E Voice rest is irrelevant to the rash.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gently but quickly assess the patient's airway with a tongue depressor

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Peritonsillar abscess: unilateral swelling pushing the uvula to the opposite side ± trismus/drooling.  Why not the others B Bacterial tonsillitis is bilateral without uvular deviation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reassurance, analgesia, bed and voice rest

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Glandular fever causes splenomegaly; contact sport → splenic rupture with haemorrhagic shock.  Why not the others J A UTI doesn't cause shock with abdominal signs after EBV.

choices.label: H
choices.text: Take the patient to the resuscitation room and contact a senior anaesthetist and ENT surgeon
choices.explanation: Acute epiglottitis — do NOT examine the throat; secure the airway with senior anaesthetic/ENT support.  Why not the others C NEVER use a tongue depressor in suspected epiglottitis — it can precipitate complete obstruction.

questions.keyPoint: Epiglottitis → resus + airway team, no throat exam.
questions.keyDifferentiator: DROOLING + tripod + stridor child = epiglottitis → resus room + anaesthetics/ENT (NEVER a tongue depressor); barking cough = croup → steeple sign; uvula displaced AWAY = quinsy; glandular fever + amoxicillin → rash; glandular fever + contact sport → splenic rupture.

rows: Resus + airway team, no tongue depressor

questions.stem: A 60-year-old (Mr Jonathan): hoarse voice for 1 day after a cough/cold, painful to cough/swallow, no smoking, no weight loss/haemoptysis, no pyrexia/nodes. Treatment?

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Exudative tonsillitis + palatal petechiae transmitted between partners = EBV (glandular fever).  Why not the others E Group A strep is possible but the petechiae + transmission favour EBV.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Whitish-grey buccal lesions with the measles prodrome (cough, coryza, conjunctivitis) = Koplik's spots.  Why not the others G Lichen planus gives lacy white striae, not the measles prodrome.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Self-limiting viral URTI in a well child → supportive care (paracetamol), not antibiotics.  Why not the others A Amoxicillin isn't indicated for a viral URTI.

choices.label: J
choices.text: Reassurance, analgesia, bed and voice rest
choices.explanation: Acute (post-viral) laryngitis → conservative care with voice rest.  Why not the others K No red flags requiring ENT referral here.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Acute post-viral hoarseness = reassurance/voice rest; viral URTI in a child = paracetamol (not antibiotics); whitish-grey BUCCAL lesions + measles prodrome = Koplik's spots; palatal PETECHIAE + transmitted sore throat = EBV; PREVERTEBRAL air-fluid level + neck tilt = retropharyngeal abscess.
