questions.stem: Most frequent cause of a FACIAL NERVE palsy.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Bell's palsy (idiopathic LMN facial palsy) is the commonest cause.  Why not the others I Stroke gives an UMN palsy with forehead sparing.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ~80% of salivary tumours arise in the parotid (and ~80% of those are pleomorphic adenoma).  Why not the others J Submandibular tumours are far fewer but more often malignant.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Pneumococcal vaccination reduces recurrent AOM.  Why not the others G Routine prophylactic antibiotics aren't the preferred answer.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Most halitosis is due to oral/dental causes (poor dental hygiene).  Why not the others J Tumours are a rare cause.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Commonest facial palsy = Bell's; commonest ENT manifestation of HIV = rhinosinusitis; pneumococcal vaccine cuts recurrent AOM; halitosis mostly from poor dental hygiene; parotid = 80% of salivary tumours.

source.paper: Master Pass ENT · Master Pass ENT

questions.stem: The most common ENT MANIFESTATION OF HIV.

choices.label: A

choices.label: C

questions.stem: Beneficial in reducing the risk of RECURRENT ACUTE OTITIS MEDIA.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Bell's palsy (idiopathic LMN facial palsy) is the commonest cause.  Why not the others I Stroke gives an UMN palsy with forehead sparing.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ~80% of salivary tumours arise in the parotid (and ~80% of those are pleomorphic adenoma).  Why not the others J Submandibular tumours are far fewer but more often malignant.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Pneumococcal vaccination reduces recurrent AOM.  Why not the others G Routine prophylactic antibiotics aren't the preferred answer.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Most halitosis is due to oral/dental causes (poor dental hygiene).  Why not the others J Tumours are a rare cause.

questions.stem: A 23-year-old lady: severe epistaxis, SLE + menorrhagia (no gynae cause found), stable, no rash; Hb 12.6, WCC 6.0, PLATELETS 60.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Post-MI antiplatelet (aspirin) predisposes to epistaxis.  Why not the others I Warfarin is also plausible given AF — see flag.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Nightclub + unilateral nasal bleeding in a young person = cocaine-induced epistaxis (mucosal vasoconstriction/necrosis).  Why not the others C Aspirin doesn't fit the nightclub/unilateral picture.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Isolated thrombocytopenia + menorrhagia in SLE = (secondary) immune thrombocytopenic purpura.  Why not the others E DIC has deranged clotting + low fibrinogen, usually unwell.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Saddle nose + renal impairment + epistaxis = granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener's); cANCA/anti-PR3 positive.  Why not the others D Cocaine causes a perforation but not renal failure.

questions.keyDifferentiator: SLE + menorrhagia + isolated low platelets = ITP; nightclub + nasal source = cocaine; Auer rods = AML; saddle nose + renal impairment = Wegener's (GPA).

source.paper: Master Pass ENT · Master Pass ENT

questions.stem: A 13-year-old boy: struggling to breathe on the sports pitch, terrible cough keeping him up at night.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Exercise-induced breathlessness + nocturnal cough = asthma.  Why not the others J Tracheomalacia presents in infancy.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Barking ('seal') cough = croup (laryngotracheobronchitis).  Why not the others C Epiglottitis has drooling/toxaemia, not a barking cough.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Tripod posture + toxaemia in an UNVACCINATED child = epiglottitis (H. influenzae b).  Why not the others B Croup has a barking cough, not tripod toxaemia.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: 'Hot-potato' muffled voice + trismus + uvula deviated away = quinsy (peritonsillar abscess).  Why not the others E Peritonsillar cellulitis precedes abscess and lacks fluctuant collection/marked deviation.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Exercise + nocturnal cough = asthma; barking cough = croup; bleeding after recent oral op = post-tonsillectomy haemorrhage; muffled voice + trismus + uvula deviated = quinsy; tripod + unvaccinated = epiglottitis.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old playing football: sudden breathlessness, chest tightening, can't complete sentences, REDUCED AIR ENTRY bilaterally.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Choking with an EFFECTIVE cough (conscious) → encourage coughing.  Why not the others D Back blows are for an INEFFECTIVE cough.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Choking with an INEFFECTIVE cough but conscious → 5 back blows (then chest/abdominal thrusts).  Why not the others C 'Encourage cough' is for an effective cough.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Give 10 mg chlorphenamine IV and 200 mg hydrocortisone IV

choices.label: F
choices.text: Give 10 mg chlorphenamine IV and 200 mg hydrocortisone PO
choices.explanation: Anaphylaxis that has responded to adrenaline → second-line antihistamine + corticosteroid.  Why not the others K Repeating adrenaline is for a patient who has NOT improved.

choices.label: I
choices.text: Nebulised O₂-driven bronchodilators and 100 mg hydrocortisone IV
choices.explanation: Acute severe asthma → O₂-driven nebulised bronchodilators + IV hydrocortisone.  Why not the others K No urticaria/angio-oedema to suggest anaphylaxis.

questions.keyPoint: Acute severe asthma = nebs + IV steroid.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Acute severe asthma = nebulised bronchodilators + IV hydrocortisone; anaphylaxis IMPROVED after adrenaline = antihistamine + steroid; anaphylaxis NOT improved = repeat adrenaline 1:1000 IM (NOT 1:10 000, which is IV cardiac-arrest); choking + effective cough = encourage cough; choking + ineffective cough (conscious) = 5 back blows.

source.paper: Master Pass ENT · Master Pass ENT

questions.stem: A 12-year-old: anaphylaxis (urticaria + lip/tongue swelling) on a school trip, given ONE adrenaline dose; on arrival the airway has IMPROVED and he maintains it alone.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Choking with an EFFECTIVE cough (conscious) → encourage coughing.  Why not the others D Back blows are for an INEFFECTIVE cough.

choices.label: E

choices.label: I

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Persisting anaphylaxis → repeat IM adrenaline 0.5 mg of 1:1000.  Why not the others J 1:10 000 IM is wrong — 1:10 000 is the IV concentration used in cardiac arrest.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Acute severe asthma = nebulised bronchodilators + IV hydrocortisone; anaphylaxis IMPROVED after adrenaline = antihistamine + steroid; anaphylaxis NOT improved = repeat adrenaline 1:1000 IM (NOT 1:10 000, which is IV cardiac-arrest); choking + effective cough = encourage cough; choking + ineffective cough (conscious) = 5 back blows.

flag.note: Marked answer gives hydrocortisone PO (F). In acute anaphylaxis hydrocortisone is usually given IV (option E); also, current Resuscitation Council UK guidance de-emphasises routine antihistamines/steroids — adrenaline is the key drug.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man: thyroid lump, normal TFTs, ELEVATED CALCITONIN.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A differentiated thyroid carcinoma (thyroglobulin-secreting); keyed here as follicular.  Why not the others B A follicular adenoma is benign; malignancy is implied by the context/marker.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Calcitonin is secreted by parafollicular C cells → medullary thyroid carcinoma.  Why not the others I Papillary uses thyroglobulin, not calcitonin.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Medullary thyroid ca + phaeochromocytoma + parathyroid hyperplasia, WITHOUT mucosal neuromas = MEN 2A.  Why not the others H MEN 2B has mucosal neuromas + marfanoid habitus.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Psammoma bodies + lymph-node spread = papillary thyroid carcinoma (commonest, good prognosis).  Why not the others C Follicular spreads haematogenously, not via nodes/psammoma bodies.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Calcitonin = medullary; elderly rapid hard nodule + dysphagia = anaplastic; psammoma bodies + nodes = papillary; thyroglobulin = differentiated (follicular/papillary); medullary + phaeo + parathyroid = MEN 2A.

choices.explanation: Calcitonin is secreted by parafollicular C cells → medullary thyroid carcinoma.  Why not the others I Papillary uses thyroglobulin, not calcitonin.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Medullary thyroid ca + phaeochromocytoma + parathyroid hyperplasia, WITHOUT mucosal neuromas = MEN 2A.  Why not the others H MEN 2B has mucosal neuromas + marfanoid habitus.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Psammoma bodies + lymph-node spread = papillary thyroid carcinoma (commonest, good prognosis).  Why not the others C Follicular spreads haematogenously, not via nodes/psammoma bodies.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Calcitonin = medullary; elderly rapid hard nodule + dysphagia = anaplastic; psammoma bodies + nodes = papillary; thyroglobulin = differentiated (follicular/papillary); medullary + phaeo + parathyroid = MEN 2A.

choices.label: B

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Epidermoid + mucous cells = mucoepidermoid carcinoma; pain + facial-nerve involvement indicate high grade.  Why not the others F Pleomorphic adenoma is painless without facial weakness.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Pleomorphic adenoma of the superficial lobe of the parotid
choices.explanation: Commonest parotid tumour: benign pleomorphic adenoma of the superficial lobe; no facial-nerve involvement.  Why not the others B Adenoid cystic is malignant with facial-nerve/perineural involvement.

choices.label: G

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Mealtime pain/swelling of a salivary gland = a duct stone (sialolithiasis).  Why not the others H Sialadenitis is infective/inflammatory, not specifically mealtime-triggered.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Mealtime pain/swelling = sialolithiasis (stone); slow painless smooth parotid lump, no facial weakness = pleomorphic adenoma; painful + facial-nerve weakness + epidermoid&mucous cells = high-grade mucoepidermoid; tail of parotid, almost never malignant = Warthin's; facial nerve sacrificed/perineural = adenoid cystic.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old: PAINFUL MIDLINE neck lump at the thyroid level, firm + TENDER, MOVES on TONGUE PROTRUSION; thick-walled mass on USS.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Posterior-triangle, present from birth, brilliantly transilluminating = cystic hygroma (lymphangioma).  Why not the others H Branchial cyst presents in young adults, anterior to SCM.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A midline cyst above the hyoid that doesn't transilluminate and isn't tethered = dermoid cyst.  Why not the others J Thyroglossal cyst moves on tongue protrusion.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A midline cyst moving on tongue protrusion that is painful/tender with a thick wall = infected thyroglossal cyst.  Why not the others J An uninfected thyroglossal cyst is painless.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: A transilluminating lateral neck cyst (anterior to SCM) in a young adult = second branchial cleft cyst.  Why not the others A Cystic hygroma is in the posterior triangle, usually from birth.

questions.keyPoint: Midline + moves on tongue protrusion + tender = infected thyroglossal cyst.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Midline, moves on TONGUE PROTRUSION (painful/tender = infected) = thyroglossal cyst; lateral neck, transilluminates = branchial cyst; midline above hyoid, no transillumination = dermoid; posterior triangle since birth = cystic hygroma; enlarges on Valsalva (COPD) = laryngocele.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old student: 2-week fatigue + fever, generalised neck swellings, AMOXICILLIN (for presumed LRTI) stopped due to a RASH; cervical lymphadenopathy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: B symptoms + Reed–Sternberg cells = Hodgkin's lymphoma.  Why not the others G NHL lacks Reed–Sternberg cells.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Chronic hypoxia (cyanotic heart disease) drives carotid-body hyperplasia; the mass is mobile side-to-side but not vertically (tethered to the carotid).  Why not the others H 'Sporadic' lacks the chronic-hypoxia association seen here.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: EBV glandular fever: lymphadenopathy + amoxicillin-induced rash.  Why not the others A Bacterial tonsillitis doesn't cause an amoxicillin rash.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Bilateral submandibular/floor-of-mouth cellulitis from a dental source + airway threat = Ludwig's angina (airway emergency).  Why not the others I A submandibular abscess is usually unilateral and localised.

questions.keyPoint: Amoxicillin rash + lymphadenopathy in a student = EBV.

questions.stem: What is the DEFINITION of apnoea?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Apnoea = cessation of airflow for ≥10 seconds.  Why not the others B That describes hypopnoea (reduced ventilation), not apnoea.

choices.label: B
choices.text: 10-second cessation of breathing with reduction of ventilation by at least 50%

choices.label: C
choices.text: 5-second cessation of breathing with reduction of ventilation by at least 50%

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A professional driver with excessive daytime sleepiness is a danger to the public — the FIRST step is to advise him to stop driving/work (and inform DVLA) before investigations.  Why not the others J Sleep studies are needed too, but patient/public SAFETY comes first.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: No respiratory effort during apnoeas = central sleep apnoea (often with cardiac failure).  Why not the others H Obstructive apnoea shows effort against obstruction.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Effort to breathe against an obstruction (struggling to shift air) = obstructive sleep apnoea.  Why not the others E Central apnoea has NO respiratory effort.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Apnoea = 10-second cessation of breathing; a professional driver with daytime somnolence must be advised to stop driving/work FIRST (safety); struggling to shift air = obstructive; no struggle during desaturations = central; CPAP when splints insufficient.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man: 1-month dysphagia + lethargy + weight loss, family say his VOICE has CHANGED recently; GORD, smokes, drinks.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Hoarseness/voice change is the hallmark of GLOTTIC (vocal cord) carcinoma — it presents early because the cord is involved; advanced disease adds dysphagia/weight loss.  Why not the others E Oesophageal cancer causes dysphagia but voice change implies laryngeal/RLN involvement.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Lace-like reticular white striae (Wickham's striae) that don't rub off = oral lichen planus.  Why not the others C Leukoplakia is a white plaque, not a lace-like reticular pattern.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Indurated, exophytic, erythroleukoplakic mouth ulcer + referred otalgia in a smoker/drinker = oral SCC.  Why not the others A Aphthous ulcers are shallow, painful, and self-limiting without induration.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Gurgling neck + aspiration + barium-diagnostic = pharyngeal pouch (Zenker's diverticulum).  Why not the others J A TOF causes coughing on swallowing rather than a barium-diagnostic pouch.

questions.keyPoint: Persistent voice change in a smoker = glottic laryngeal carcinoma.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman: gradual bilateral SNHL, audiogram FLAT at 40 dB across ALL frequencies, NO vertigo.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Age-related down-sloping (high-frequency) bilateral SNHL = presbycusis.  Why not the others B Noise-induced loss notches at 4 kHz rather than down-sloping.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A symmetrical flat SNHL in a young adult without other features suggests genetic/familial hearing loss.  Why not the others C Presbycusis is age-related and high-frequency.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A sudden unilateral SNHL must prompt MRI to exclude a vestibular schwannoma (the unilateral cause among these options).  Why not the others C Presbycusis is gradual and bilateral.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Symmetrical flat SNHL in a young adult, no vertigo = genetic/familial; SUDDEN unilateral SNHL / dead ear = exclude acoustic neuroma; elderly down-sloping bilateral SNHL = presbycusis.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman: gradual LEFT conductive loss, air–bone gap 20 dB across all frequencies with BONE-CONDUCTION dip/closure at 4 kHz; TYPE A tympanogram.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Young woman + conductive loss + Carhart's-type notch + normal (type A) tympanogram = otosclerosis (stapes fixation).  Why not the others C Glue ear gives a type B/flat tympanogram.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Painless conductive loss after a URTI = otitis media with effusion (glue ear).  Why not the others B ASOM is acutely painful with a bulging red TM.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: A large conductive loss after head trauma = ossicular (incudostapedial) dislocation.  Why not the others A Otosclerosis is gradual, not post-traumatic.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman: over 4 years, three attacks of debilitating vertigo lasting a few hours, each preceded by AURAL FULLNESS and TINNITUS in the left ear.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Brief positional vertigo on turning in bed = BPPV (the symmetrical SNHL is age-related/incidental).  Why not the others C Ménière lasts hours with aural fullness.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Multidirectional (gaze-evoked) nystagmus + ataxia + normal hearing = a CENTRAL cause.  Why not the others A BPPV has positional torsional nystagmus and normal coordination.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Episodic vertigo (hours) preceded by aural fullness + tinnitus = Ménière.  Why not the others A BPPV lasts seconds and is positional.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Progressive unsteadiness + asymmetrical SNHL + unilateral tinnitus = acoustic neuroma → MRI IAM.  Why not the others C Ménière is episodic with fluctuating low-frequency SNHL.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy whose parents are concerned about his hearing.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A cooperative school-age child can perform pure-tone audiometry.  Why not the others D Play audiometry is for younger children.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Objective thresholds for cochlear-implant candidacy in an infant = auditory brainstem response.  Why not the others D Play audiometry isn't possible at 7 months.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Play audiometry is the age-appropriate behavioural test for a 2–4-year-old.  Why not the others A Pure-tone audiometry needs an older, cooperative child.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Oto-acoustic emissions are the universal newborn hearing SCREEN.  Why not the others C ABR is used to confirm/obtain thresholds after a failed screen.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Toddler (2–4 yr) = play audiometry; newborn SCREENING = oto-acoustic emissions; objective thresholds for cochlear-implant work-up = auditory brainstem response; school-age child = pure-tone audiogram.

questions.stem: Weber localises to the RIGHT; Rinne POSITIVE on both sides.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Right Rinne negative + Weber to the right (affected side) = right conductive loss.  Why not the others F SNHL would have Rinne positive and Weber to the better ear.

choices.label: F

questions.keyDifferentiator: Weber lateralises to the BETTER ear in SNHL and to the AFFECTED ear in conductive loss; Rinne positive = AC>BC (normal/SNHL), Rinne negative = conductive on that side.

choices.explanation: Acute mastoiditis (postauricular swelling pushing the pinna down/forward + fever) → admit for IV antibiotics ± cortical mastoidectomy.  Why not the others G Oral amoxicillin is insufficient once mastoiditis has developed.

questions.stem: A 21-year-old man: soft 3 cm mass JUST ANTERIOR to the SCM at the junction of the upper/middle thirds; does NOT move on swallowing or transilluminate.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Midline cyst moving on tongue protrusion = thyroglossal cyst.  Why not the others J A goitre moves on swallowing, not tongue protrusion.

choices.label: E

questions.stem: A 6-month-old (FORCEPS delivery): head PERMANENTLY TILTED to the left with a lump in the SCM.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: An SCM 'tumour'/fibrosis after birth trauma = congenital torticollis.  Why not the others F Branchial cyst presents in young adults.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Midline cyst moving on tongue protrusion = thyroglossal cyst.  Why not the others J A goitre moves on swallowing, not tongue protrusion.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Gurgling + regurgitation/aspiration = pharyngeal pouch.  Why not the others J Goitre moves on swallowing without gurgling/aspiration.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Classic site/character of a (second) branchial cleft cyst.  Why not the others C Cystic hygroma transilluminates and is posterior-triangle.

questions.keyPoint: Infant SCM lump + head tilt = torticollis.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Anterior to SCM at upper/middle third, no transillumination = branchial cyst; SCM lump + tilted head in an infant = torticollis; gurgling + undigested food + aspiration = pharyngeal pouch; pulsatile mass = carotid body tumour; midline moving on tongue protrusion = thyroglossal cyst.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old after assault with a suspected ISOLATED NASAL FRACTURE.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Culture and sensitivity of nasal mucus

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Pre-FESS planning uses a non-contrast sinus CT.  Why not the others D Contrast is reserved for tumours/vascular lesions.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Suspected juvenile angiofibroma → contrast CT (assess vascularity/extent); biopsy avoided.  Why not the others C A vascular tumour needs contrast imaging.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mucosal biopsy and blood test for ESR and ANCA
choices.explanation: Nasal + renal + lung features suggest GPA → mucosal biopsy + ESR/ANCA.  Why not the others F Lavage doesn't diagnose vasculitis.

choices.label: G
choices.text: Biopsy under endoscopic control in clinic

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Simple nasal fractures need no acute imaging — review at 5–7 days for manipulation once swelling settles.  Why not the others B Facial X-rays don't change management of a simple nasal fracture.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Isolated nasal fracture → review in 5–7 days (no imaging); pre-FESS polyps → non-contrast CT; unilateral mass/?angiofibroma → contrast CT; resistant maxillary sinusitis → antral lavage; ?GPA → biopsy + ESR/ANCA.

questions.stem: Which vessel is most likely to cause significant POST-TRAUMATIC epistaxis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Post-traumatic (superior) epistaxis classically arises from the anterior ethmoidal artery.  Why not the others C Sphenopalatine bleeds are the typical posterior spontaneous source.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: The maxillary (and frontal/anterior ethmoid) sinuses drain into the middle meatus.  Why not the others D The inferior meatus carries the nasolacrimal duct.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Posterior ethmoid cells drain into the superior meatus (the sphenoid drains into the sphenoethmoidal recess).  Why not the others E Anterior ethmoid cells drain into the middle meatus.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: The nasolacrimal duct opens into the inferior meatus.  Why not the others E The middle meatus drains the maxillary/frontal/anterior ethmoid sinuses.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Post-traumatic epistaxis = anterior ethmoidal artery; maxillary ostium drains to middle meatus; periorbital cellulitis spreads from the ethmoid sinus; nasolacrimal duct opens in the inferior meatus; posterior ethmoid drains to the superior meatus.

source.paper: ENT Textbook — Ch43 Nose & Sinuses · Ch43 Nose

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: The nasolacrimal duct opens into the inferior meatus.  Why not the others E The middle meatus drains the maxillary/frontal/anterior ethmoid sinuses.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Orbital cellulitis usually spreads from the ethmoid sinus through the lamina papyracea.  Why not the others H The maxillary sinus is below the orbit, a less common route.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old: pyrexia + systemic upset + a BULGING RED ear drum.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prescribe analgesia with systemic antibiotics if symptoms do not settle within 48 h
choices.explanation: AOM → analgesia with a delayed-antibiotic strategy (antibiotics if not settling in 48 h).  Why not the others E IV antibiotics are for complicated/malignant disease.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Active (safe) CSOM → aural toilet + topical antibiotic/steroid drops.  Why not the others G Surgery to exteriorise is for cholesteatoma.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Topical antibiotic drops with a prolonged course of intravenous (IV) antibiotics
choices.explanation: Malignant (necrotising) otitis externa → topical drops + prolonged IV anti-pseudomonal antibiotics.  Why not the others D Simple drops are insufficient for skull-base osteomyelitis.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Surgical repair of the ear drum

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Cholesteatoma → mastoid surgery to exteriorise/clear the disease.  Why not the others D Drops don't eradicate cholesteatoma.

choices.label: H
choices.text: Urgent contrast-enhanced CT scan of temporal bones and brain

choices.label: I
choices.text: Plain X-ray of the affected temporal bone

questions.keyDifferentiator: AOM = analgesia + delayed antibiotics; otitis externa = topical antibiotic/steroid drops; malignant OE (diabetic + facial palsy) = topical + prolonged IV antibiotics; cholesteatoma = surgery to exteriorise; reduced GCS after otitis = urgent CT brain/temporal bones.

questions.stem: Where does the FACIAL NERVE enter the temporal bone?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The auricle forms from the six hillocks of His on the 1st and 2nd branchial arches.  Why not the others B 2nd/3rd arches are not the auricular source.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Geniculate-ganglion zoster of CN VII produces EAC/concha vesicles (sensory branch).  Why not the others D CN VIII is sensory hearing/balance, not the canal skin.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: The vagus (CN X, via its auricular branch — Arnold's nerve) refers laryngeal pain to the ear.  Why not the others C CN VII supplies a smaller part of the canal.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: CN VII enters the temporal bone via the internal acoustic meatus (with CN VIII).  Why not the others F It EXITS at the stylomastoid foramen.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Facial nerve enters via the internal acoustic meatus; otosclerosis affects the stapes; the external ear develops from the 1st/2nd arches; Ramsay Hunt EAC vesicles are via CN VII; referred otalgia in laryngeal cancer is via CN X (vagus).

source.paper: ENT Textbook — Ch44 The Ear · Ch44 Ear

questions.stem: Which area is most commonly affected by OTOSCLEROSIS?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The auricle forms from the six hillocks of His on the 1st and 2nd branchial arches.  Why not the others B 2nd/3rd arches are not the auricular source.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Geniculate-ganglion zoster of CN VII produces EAC/concha vesicles (sensory branch).  Why not the others D CN VIII is sensory hearing/balance, not the canal skin.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: The vagus (CN X, via its auricular branch — Arnold's nerve) refers laryngeal pain to the ear.  Why not the others C CN VII supplies a smaller part of the canal.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Otosclerosis fixes the stapes footplate at the oval window.  Why not the others H The incus is less commonly the site.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Facial nerve enters via the internal acoustic meatus; otosclerosis affects the stapes; the external ear develops from the 1st/2nd arches; Ramsay Hunt EAC vesicles are via CN VII; referred otalgia in laryngeal cancer is via CN X (vagus).

source.paper: ENT Textbook — Ch44 The Ear · Ch44 Ear

choices.label: C

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: The vagus (CN X, via its auricular branch — Arnold's nerve) refers laryngeal pain to the ear.  Why not the others C CN VII supplies a smaller part of the canal.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: CN VII enters the temporal bone via the internal acoustic meatus (with CN VIII).  Why not the others F It EXITS at the stylomastoid foramen.

questions.topic: Investigation of the Pharynx, Larynx & Neck
questions.optionsTitle: Investigation of the Pharynx, Larynx & Neck

source.paper: ENT Textbook — Ch45 Pharynx/Larynx/Neck · Ch45 PLN

questions.stem: A 72-year-old: aspiration pneumonia + reflux of UNDIGESTED food hours after meals.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A neck node → fine-needle aspiration cytology (avoid open biopsy of a possible nodal metastasis).  Why not the others B Open excision risks seeding and disrupts a neck dissection plane.

choices.label: D
choices.text: CT scan of the neck and chest
choices.explanation: A new cord palsy needs CT of the neck AND chest to trace the recurrent laryngeal nerve (lung apex/mediastinum).  Why not the others H Imaging the whole RLN course precedes EUA here.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A carotid body tumour (transversely mobile, pulsatile) is characterised on MRI/MRA.  Why not the others A FNA of a vascular carotid body tumour is contraindicated.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: A pharyngeal pouch is best shown on a barium swallow.  Why not the others C Ultrasound doesn't demonstrate a pouch.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Pharyngeal pouch → barium swallow; cervical node → FNA; carotid body tumour (mobile transverse only) → MRI; ulcerated cord lesion → EUA/panendoscopy + biopsy; new cord palsy → CT neck + chest (trace the RLN).

questions.stem: A stertorous 16-year-old with tonsillitis + positive glandular-fever test failing antibiotics/fluids/analgesia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Quinsy (peritonsillar abscess) → per-oral aspiration / incision & drainage.  Why not the others I Steroids alone don't drain an abscess.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Laryngeal trauma with progressive obstruction → early tracheostomy (avoid blind intubation).  Why not the others D Intubation may worsen a laryngeal fracture/false passage.

choices.label: G
choices.text: Transfer to theatre with experienced anaesthetist and surgeon
choices.explanation: Epiglottitis → controlled intubation in theatre with senior anaesthetic + ENT cover (do not distress the child).  Why not the others D Don't attempt intubation outside a controlled setting.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Severe EBV tonsillitis with airway-compromising swelling → a short course of corticosteroids.  Why not the others B There's no abscess to drain.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Glandular-fever tonsillitis with airway compromise = steroids; epiglottitis = controlled airway in theatre with anaesthetist/ENT; quinsy = per-oral incision & drainage; laryngeal trauma with rising stridor = early tracheostomy; laryngeal papillomatosis = endoscopic excision.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old FARMER: visual deterioration; a thickened conjunctival INGROWTH with prominent vessels covering part of the cornea.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Painful red eye + fixed mid-dilated pupil = acute angle-closure glaucoma (emergency).  Why not the others H Episcleritis is not painful with a fixed pupil.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: AS (HLA-B27) + circumcorneal injection + AC cells = anterior uveitis.  Why not the others D Bacterial conjunctivitis lacks AC cells/photophobia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Bilateral gritty papillary reaction with mucopurulent discharge = bacterial conjunctivitis.  Why not the others L Viral is watery with follicles + preauricular node.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: UV/wind exposure (farmer) → fibrovascular pterygium encroaching on the cornea.  Why not the others L Viral keratoconjunctivitis is acute and infective, not a fibrovascular growth.

questions.keyPoint: Farmer + conjunctival ingrowth onto cornea = pterygium.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Fixed mid-dilated pupil + painful red eye = acute angle-closure glaucoma; preauricular node + watery + subepithelial corneal opacities = viral keratoconjunctivitis; AS + AC cells = anterior uveitis; penetrating (irregular pupil, soft eye) vs blunt (hyphema, raised IOP) globe injury.

source.paper: Ophthalmology App · App: Eye Disorders

questions.stem: A CARPENTER's eye injured by a NAIL: redness, IRREGULAR pupil, SHALLOW anterior chamber, SOFT eye.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Painful red eye + fixed mid-dilated pupil = acute angle-closure glaucoma (emergency).  Why not the others H Episcleritis is not painful with a fixed pupil.

choices.label: C

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: A localised, sectoral, mildly tender redness away from the limbus = episcleritis.  Why not the others D Conjunctivitis is diffuse with discharge.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Irregular pupil + shallow AC + hypotony after a sharp injury = open-globe (penetrating) injury.  Why not the others G A corneal abrasion is superficial without a distorted pupil/soft eye.

questions.keyPoint: Irregular pupil + shallow AC + soft eye = open globe.

choices.label: B

questions.stem: A 70-year-old with NO DM/HTN: gradual painless visual loss.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A ≥1 dioptre difference in spherical equivalent between the eyes = anisometropia.  Why not the others D Keratoconus shows irregular astigmatism/corneal thinning.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Gradual painless loss in an elderly non-diabetic = cataract.  Why not the others E Glaucoma loses peripheral field first, often unnoticed.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Sudden painless loss + cherry-red spot = central retinal artery occlusion.  Why not the others B Cataract is gradual, not sudden.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Large C/D + peripheral field loss with preserved central VA = open-angle glaucoma.  Why not the others B Cataract reduces central VA early.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Gradual painless visual loss in an elderly non-diabetic = cataract; near difficulty with normal distance VA = presbyopia; >1 D difference between eyes = anisometropia; large C/D + peripheral field loss = open-angle glaucoma; progressive myopia + family keratoplasty = keratoconus; cherry-red spot = CRAO. PITS: lower (inferior) quadrantanopia = PARIETAL lobe.

source.paper: Ophthalmology App · App: Ocular Motility

questions.stem: ANISOCORIA (unequal pupils).

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Parasympathetic fibres run with CN III; a CN III lesion gives a dilated pupil → anisocoria.  Why not the others C CN VI is purely motor to the lateral rectus, with no pupil effect.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CN IV (superior oblique) palsy → vertical diplopia worse on downgaze/reading; patients tilt the head away.  Why not the others C CN VI causes horizontal diplopia on lateral gaze.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CN VI palsy weakens the lateral rectus → unopposed medial rectus → esotropia + failure of abduction.  Why not the others B CN IV affects vertical movement, not horizontal esotropia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CN VII palsy reduces lacrimation and blink/lid closure → exposure dry eye.  Why not the others E Optic neuropathy affects vision, not tear film.

questions.keyDifferentiator: CN III → ptosis + 'down-and-out' eye + dilated pupil; CN IV → vertical diplopia worse looking DOWN/reading (superior oblique); CN VI → esotropia (lateral rectus); CN VII → dry eye (reduced lacrimation/blink).

flag.note: The app labels this 'bilateral oculomotor dysfunction', but anisocoria is by definition asymmetric — a UNILATERAL CN III palsy classically gives a fixed dilated pupil on one side.

source.paper: Ophthalmology App · App: Ocular Motility

questions.stem: ESOTROPIA (inward deviation of the eye).

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CN III supplies the levator palpebrae superioris → ptosis.  Why not the others D CN VII closes the eye (orbicularis), it doesn't elevate the lid.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CN IV (superior oblique) palsy → vertical diplopia worse on downgaze/reading; patients tilt the head away.  Why not the others C CN VI causes horizontal diplopia on lateral gaze.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CN VI palsy weakens the lateral rectus → unopposed medial rectus → esotropia + failure of abduction.  Why not the others B CN IV affects vertical movement, not horizontal esotropia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CN VII palsy reduces lacrimation and blink/lid closure → exposure dry eye.  Why not the others E Optic neuropathy affects vision, not tear film.

questions.keyPoint: Esotropia + failed abduction = CN VI palsy.

questions.stem: An elderly woman attending for a ROUTINE ophthalmic check related to her SYSTEMIC MEDICATION (risk of dry eye/scleritis & drug retinopathy screening).

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Anti-VEGF treats diabetic macular oedema/proliferative diabetic retinopathy.  Why not the others B Hypertensive retinopathy is usually managed by BP control.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A painless subconjunctival haemorrhage is commonly associated with hypertension.  Why not the others E Ankylosing spondylitis causes painful uveitis, not a painless patch.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: RA causes dry eye/scleritis and needs ophthalmic monitoring (e.g. hydroxychloroquine retinopathy screening).  Why not the others A Diabetic screening is retinopathy-focused, not dry eye/scleritis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Lid retraction + restrictive diplopia = thyroid eye disease (Graves').  Why not the others C RA doesn't cause lid retraction/proptosis.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Lid retraction + diplopia = Graves'; anterior uveitis in a young man = ankylosing spondylitis (HLA-B27); painless subconjunctival red patch = hypertension; anti-VEGF for retinopathy = diabetes; routine drug-toxicity screening (e.g. hydroxychloroquine) + dry eye/scleritis = rheumatoid arthritis.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old T2DM: retinal MICROANEURYSMS, haemorrhages and HARD EXUDATES; NO visual impairment.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Microaneurysms + dot/blot haemorrhages + hard exudates without visual loss = background (mild non-proliferative) retinopathy.  Why not the others H Proliferative disease has new vessels.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Reduced central VA with only background changes = diabetic macular oedema.  Why not the others A Background retinopathy alone doesn't reduce acuity.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: New vessels (NVD/NVE) define proliferative diabetic retinopathy → needs pan-retinal photocoagulation/anti-VEGF.  Why not the others G Pre-proliferative has cotton-wool spots but no new vessels.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Flashes/floaters + a descending curtain = retinal detachment (often tractional in diabetes).  Why not the others J Vitreous haemorrhage clouds vision but lacks the curtain/flashes pattern.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Microaneurysms/dot-blot haemorrhages/hard exudates + normal vision = background; reduced VA with background changes = macular oedema; COTTON-WOOL SPOTS = pre-proliferative; NEW VESSELS = proliferative; flashes/floaters + curtain = retinal detachment.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman: worsening headaches, intermittent double vision MOST NOTICEABLE looking towards the LEFT.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Horizontal diplopia worse on left gaze = left abducens (CN VI) palsy (weak lateral rectus); raised ICP is a false-localising cause.  Why not the others H CN IV gives vertical diplopia worse on downgaze.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A 'down-and-out' frozen eye = oculomotor (CN III) palsy (classically with ptosis ± a dilated pupil).  Why not the others A CN VI affects only abduction, not a down-and-out eye.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Bitemporal hemianopia from chiasmal compression (pituitary macroadenoma, here acromegaly).  Why not the others E An optic tract lesion gives a homonymous (not bitemporal) defect.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Right temporal + left nasal field loss = LEFT homonymous hemianopia → a right retrochiasmal (optic tract) lesion.  Why not the others C A chiasm lesion gives bitemporal loss.

questions.keyPoint: Horizontal diplopia worse to one side = CN VI palsy.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Horizontal diplopia worse on gaze to the affected side = CN VI; bitemporal hemianopia = chiasm; 'down-and-out' frozen eye = CN III; homonymous hemianopia = optic tract; SUPERIOR homonymous quadrantanopia = temporal lobe (PITS).

rows: Horizontal diplopia worse to one side

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man: blurry vision, difficulty FOCUSING when reading; fundoscopy unremarkable.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Transient monocular visual loss that resolves = amaurosis fugax (embolic TIA) → investigate carotids.  Why not the others G Homonymous hemianopia is a fixed binocular field defect.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Glare + reduced red reflex + gradual loss = cataract.  Why not the others H Hypermetropia has a normal red reflex.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A discrete central field defect = central scotoma (e.g. optic neuritis in a young woman).  Why not the others A Amaurosis fugax is transient, not a fixed patch.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Long-sightedness (hypermetropia) gives near blur, worse as accommodation declines with age; fundus is normal.  Why not the others C Cataract reduces the red reflex/clarity.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Near blur with normal fundus in older adult = hypermetropia; transient monocular loss resolving = amaurosis fugax; bilateral loss with DENIAL of blindness = cortical blindness (Anton); glare at night + reduced red reflex = cataract; a discrete dark central patch = central scotoma.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman: a DARK PATCH of visual loss within the field of the RIGHT eye; rest of vision fine.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Transient monocular visual loss that resolves = amaurosis fugax (embolic TIA) → investigate carotids.  Why not the others G Homonymous hemianopia is a fixed binocular field defect.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Glare + reduced red reflex + gradual loss = cataract.  Why not the others H Hypermetropia has a normal red reflex.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A discrete central field defect = central scotoma (e.g. optic neuritis in a young woman).  Why not the others A Amaurosis fugax is transient, not a fixed patch.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Bilateral occipital damage with denial of blindness = cortical blindness (Anton's syndrome).  Why not the others A Amaurosis fugax is transient and monocular.

questions.keyPoint: Discrete central field loss = central scotoma.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Near blur with normal fundus in older adult = hypermetropia; transient monocular loss resolving = amaurosis fugax; bilateral loss with DENIAL of blindness = cortical blindness (Anton); glare at night + reduced red reflex = cataract; a discrete dark central patch = central scotoma.

source.paper: Ophthalmology Get Ahead! Medicine · Get Ahead Medicine

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman (fell, hit head, now well): ONE pupil more DILATED, NOT reacting to light, but ACCOMMODATION INTACT; no other deficit.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Small pupils that accommodate but don't react to light (light-near dissociation) = Argyll Robertson pupil (neurosyphilis/tabes).  Why not the others G Opiate miosis still reacts to light.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A tonic dilated pupil with poor light reaction but preserved accommodation, in a young woman = Holmes–Adie pupil (benign).  Why not the others F CN III palsy adds ptosis + a 'down-and-out' eye.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Ptosis + miosis (± anhidrosis) from a Pancoast (apical lung) tumour = Horner's syndrome.  Why not the others F CN III palsy gives a DILATED pupil + 'down-and-out' eye.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A relative afferent pupillary defect (swinging-flashlight test) = Marcus Gunn pupil (here from optic neuritis in MS).  Why not the others C Holmes–Adie is an efferent tonic pupil, not an afferent defect.

questions.keyPoint: Tonic dilated pupil + intact accommodation = Holmes–Adie.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Dilated tonic pupil + poor light reaction + intact accommodation in a young woman = Holmes–Adie; pinpoint pupils + respiratory depression = opiates; ptosis + miosis = Horner's; RAPD (dilates on swinging light) = Marcus Gunn; small pupils with light-near dissociation (accommodates, no light reaction) = Argyll Robertson.

source.paper: Ophthalmology Get Ahead! Medicine · Get Ahead Medicine

questions.stem: A 64-year-old post-op (hip replacement): unresponsive 12 h after surgery, SMALL pupils, respiratory rate 7/min.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Small pupils that accommodate but don't react to light (light-near dissociation) = Argyll Robertson pupil (neurosyphilis/tabes).  Why not the others G Opiate miosis still reacts to light.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A tonic dilated pupil with poor light reaction but preserved accommodation, in a young woman = Holmes–Adie pupil (benign).  Why not the others F CN III palsy adds ptosis + a 'down-and-out' eye.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Ptosis + miosis (± anhidrosis) from a Pancoast (apical lung) tumour = Horner's syndrome.  Why not the others F CN III palsy gives a DILATED pupil + 'down-and-out' eye.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Pinpoint pupils + respiratory depression + reduced consciousness = opioid toxicity (give naloxone).  Why not the others D Horner's gives unilateral miosis, not bilateral pinpoint with respiratory depression.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Dilated tonic pupil + poor light reaction + intact accommodation in a young woman = Holmes–Adie; pinpoint pupils + respiratory depression = opiates; ptosis + miosis = Horner's; RAPD (dilates on swinging light) = Marcus Gunn; small pupils with light-near dissociation (accommodates, no light reaction) = Argyll Robertson.

source.paper: Ophthalmology Irfan Surgery · Irfan Surgery

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man, CATARACT SURGERY ~1 week ago: painful red congested eye, discharge, HYPOPYON and reduced red reflex; no fundus view.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Atopy (eczema) + itch + stringy discharge ± corneal involvement = atopic keratoconjunctivitis.  Why not the others D A subtarsal foreign body gives vertical corneal abrasions, not atopy.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Painful red eye with hypopyon soon after intraocular surgery = (post-operative) endophthalmitis — a sight-threatening emergency (vitreous tap + intravitreal antibiotics).  Why not the others L Angle-closure has a fixed mid-dilated pupil, not post-op hypopyon.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Acute mucocutaneous illness with pseudomembranous conjunctivitis = Stevens–Johnson syndrome.  Why not the others I Cicatricial pemphigoid is a chronic scarring disease of the elderly.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Hyperope + headache/vomiting + fixed mid-dilated pupil + very high IOP = acute angle-closure glaucoma.  Why not the others B Uveitis gives a small/irregular pupil with normal/low IOP.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Post-cataract painful red eye + HYPOPYON = endophthalmitis; mucocutaneous (oral ulcers/cold sores) + pseudomembranes = Stevens–Johnson; eczema + stringy discharge + corneal ulcer = atopic conjunctivitis; hyperope + fixed mid-dilated pupil + IOP 50 = acute angle closure.

source.paper: Ophthalmology Irfan Surgery · Irfan Surgery

questions.stem: A 5-year-old: learning difficulties, bilateral cataracts, EPICANTHIC FOLDS, BRUSHFIELD SPOTS and BLUE-DOT cataracts.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Brushfield spots + epicanthic folds + blue-dot cataracts = Down syndrome.  Why not the others B Marfan causes lens dislocation, not blue-dot cataracts.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Reducing (non-glucose) substance in urine + cataracts in infancy = galactosaemia (oil-droplet cataract).  Why not the others D Down has Brushfield spots, not a urinary reducing substance.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Sunflower cataract (± Kayser–Fleischer rings) + liver + neuro signs = Wilson's disease.  Why not the others J Myotonic dystrophy lacks hepatosplenomegaly/KF rings.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Frontal balding + myotonia/wasting + iridescent 'Christmas-tree' cataract = myotonic dystrophy.  Why not the others H Wilson's gives a sunflower cataract with liver/neuro signs.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Brushfield spots + blue-dot cataract = Down; frontal balding + muscle wasting + Christmas-tree cataract = myotonic dystrophy; sunflower cataract + hepatosplenomegaly/tremor = Wilson's; non-glucose reducing substance in urine = galactosaemia.

source.paper: Ophthalmology Irfan Surgery · Irfan Surgery

questions.stem: A 50-year-old smoker: left MIOSIS + PTOSIS + ANHIDROSIS, APICAL opacity (left upper lobe); after cocaine + adrenaline 1:1000 the left pupil REMAINS UNDILATED.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A relative afferent pupillary defect (abnormal swinging-light test) from a large retinal detachment = Marcus Gunn pupil.  Why not the others A An Adie pupil is a tonic efferent pupil, not an afferent defect.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Fixed dilated pupil + ptosis + 'down-and-out' eye = oculomotor (III) nerve palsy (pupil involvement mandates excluding a posterior communicating aneurysm).  Why not the others H Traumatic mydriasis has no ptosis or eye-movement defect.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Small irregular pupils with light-near dissociation + positive VDRL = Argyll Robertson pupil (neurosyphilis).  Why not the others A Adie is unilateral and tonic, not small/irregular bilaterally.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A Pancoast tumour interrupts the 2nd-order (preganglionic) sympathetic neuron; failure to dilate with adrenaline 1:1000 means NO denervation supersensitivity → pre- (not post-) ganglionic.  Why not the others F Postganglionic Horner's DOES dilate to adrenaline 1:1000 (supersensitivity).

questions.keyPoint: Pancoast Horner's, no adrenaline dilation = preganglionic.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Apical lung lesion (Pancoast) + Horner's that does NOT dilate to adrenaline 1:1000 = PREganglionic; RAPD/abnormal swinging-light + retinal detachment = Marcus Gunn; small irregular pupils + VDRL+ = Argyll Robertson; fixed dilated pupil + ptosis + down-and-out = III nerve palsy.

source.paper: Ophthalmology Irfan Surgery · Irfan Surgery

questions.stem: A 52-year-old: NIGHT BLINDNESS, SPICULE pigmentation, pale discs, arteriolar narrowing; BILATERAL CONSTRICTED fields.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Same-sided field loss in both eyes from a retrochiasmal lesion = homonymous hemianopia.  Why not the others H Bitemporal loss localises to the chiasm.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Optic neuritis (MS) classically produces a central scotoma with impaired colour vision and an RAPD.  Why not the others E Tunnel vision is concentric, not central.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Retinitis pigmentosa progressively constricts the peripheral field = tunnel vision.  Why not the others A Homonymous hemianopia is a one-sided field loss, not concentric.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Chiasmal compression (pituitary macroadenoma → acromegaly) gives bitemporal hemianopia.  Why not the others A Homonymous loss is same-sided, not bitemporal.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Retinitis pigmentosa (spicule pigment, night blindness) = tunnel vision; optic neuritis (MS, RAPD, disc swelling) = central scotoma; stroke with one-sided field loss in both eyes = homonymous hemianopia; pituitary/acromegaly = bitemporal hemianopia.

source.paper: Ophthalmology Irfan Surgery · Irfan Surgery

questions.stem: A 32-year-old poked by a branch: painful photophobic eye; a topical drop is applied to DIAGNOSE A CORNEAL ABRASION.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Pilocarpine (a miotic) pulls the iris from the angle to reopen drainage in acute angle closure.  Why not the others G Atropine DILATES and would worsen angle closure.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: IV acetazolamide (a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor) rapidly lowers aqueous production/IOP in an acute attack.  Why not the others F Pilocarpine is the topical adjunct, not the IV agent.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Edrophonium (Tensilon) is a short-acting anticholinesterase that transiently improves myasthenic weakness.  Why not the others F Pilocarpine is a topical miotic, not used for the Tensilon test.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Fluorescein stains an epithelial defect (abrasion/ulcer) green under cobalt-blue light.  Why not the others G Atropine is a cycloplegic, not a stain.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Diagnose a corneal abrasion = fluorescein; Tensilon (myasthenia) test = edrophonium; topical reversal of acute angle closure = pilocarpine; IV agent to crash the IOP = acetazolamide.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old after ASSAULT: periorbital bruising, right ptosis, ENOPHTHALMOS, restricted UP-GAZE, numb right cheek.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Ptosis + miosis with a cluster-headache pattern = Horner's syndrome.  Why not the others B III nerve palsy gives a DILATED pupil + down-and-out eye.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Fatiguable variable ptosis improving with ice (the ice-pack test) = myasthenia gravis.  Why not the others I Levator dehiscence is static, not fatiguable.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Orbital floor (blow-out) fracture → enophthalmos, restricted up-gaze (inferior rectus tethering) and infraorbital-nerve numbness.  Why not the others C Horner's gives miosis, not enophthalmos with restricted up-gaze.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Aponeurotic (levator) dehiscence → ptosis with preserved levator function and a high lid crease (typical of the elderly).  Why not the others E Myasthenic ptosis is fatiguable with poor levator.

questions.keyPoint: Enophthalmos + restricted up-gaze + cheek numbness = blow-out fracture.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Trauma + enophthalmos + restricted UP-gaze + cheek numbness = blow-out fracture; FATIGUABLE asymmetrical ptosis relieved by ICE = myasthenia; symmetrical ptosis with GOOD levator + high crease = levator dehiscence; ptosis + miosis + cluster headache = Horner's.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old FEBRILE child: swelling, ptosis and redness of the right eyelids with WARMTH of the overlying skin.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A pigmented nodular lesion with atypical melanocytes = melanoma.  Why not the others A BCC is pearly with telangiectasia, not pigmented melanocytic atypia.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Lid retraction + proptosis + restrictive myopathy + raised IOP = thyroid eye disease (Graves' orbitopathy).  Why not the others L Orbital cellulitis is acutely febrile/infective.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Post-traumatic pulsatile proptosis + bruit + dilated 'corkscrew' vessels + raised IOP + VI palsy = carotid–cavernous fistula.  Why not the others H Thyroid eye disease has no bruit/post-traumatic onset.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Febrile child with a warm, red, swollen, tender orbit = orbital cellulitis (assess for proptosis/painful eye movements).  Why not the others H Thyroid eye disease is non-infective and bilateral.

questions.keyPoint: Febrile + warm red lids = orbital cellulitis.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Febrile child + warm red swollen lids = orbital cellulitis; grittiness + lid retraction + chemosis + raised IOP = thyroid eye disease; blue-black eyelid lesion + atypical melanocytes = melanoma; post-RTA pulsatile proptosis + bruit + dilated vessels + VI palsy = carotid–cavernous fistula.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old smoker: SUDDEN PAINLESS loss of vision (right), RAPD; TORTUOUS VENOUS DILATATION, FLAME and DOT haemorrhages, cotton-wool spots.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: 'Blood-and-thunder' fundus (tortuous veins, widespread flame/dot haemorrhages, cotton-wool spots) = CRVO.  Why not the others D CRAO gives a pale retina + cherry-red spot, not haemorrhages.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Flashes + floaters + a progressing shadow/curtain in a myope = retinal detachment.  Why not the others B A macular hole gives central distortion, not a peripheral curtain.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: New vessels (NVD/NVE) = proliferative diabetic retinopathy (pregnancy accelerates it).  Why not the others J Diabetic maculopathy is macular oedema/exudate without new vessels.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Disc swelling + macular star + arteriolar narrowing = accelerated (grade IV) hypertensive retinopathy.  Why not the others A CRVO lacks the macular star/arteriolar attenuation pattern.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Tortuous dilated veins + flame & dot haemorrhages + cotton-wool spots = CRVO; arteriolar narrowing + flame haemorrhages + disc swelling + MACULAR STAR = hypertensive; flashes/floaters/shadow in a myope = detachment; new vessels at disc/retina = proliferative DR.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old: apparent bilateral DISC SWELLING; CT shows RADIO-OPAQUE opacities at the optic nerves; unclear disc margins.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Calcified (radio-opaque on CT) buried drusen mimic disc swelling = pseudopapilloedema.  Why not the others E True papilloedema has no calcific opacities on CT.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Obese + headache + bilateral disc swelling with enlarged blind spots = papilloedema (consider idiopathic intracranial hypertension).  Why not the others A Drusen are calcified on CT and not associated with headache/raised ICP.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: A pale disc after resolved optic neuritis = optic atrophy.  Why not the others E Papilloedema is acute swelling, not chronic pallor.

choices.label: M
choices.explanation: Raised IOP with an enlarged cup-to-disc ratio = glaucomatous optic disc cupping.  Why not the others E Papilloedema fills in the cup rather than enlarging it.

questions.keyDifferentiator: 'Disc swelling' + RADIO-OPAQUE (calcified) discs on CT = drusen (pseudopapilloedema); prior optic neuritis (MS) with pale disc = optic atrophy; high IOP + glaucomatous disc = cupping; obese + headache + enlarged blind spot = papilloedema.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old Afro-Caribbean woman: decreased vision, haloes, IOP 40/33; the characteristic glaucomatous disc.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Calcified (radio-opaque on CT) buried drusen mimic disc swelling = pseudopapilloedema.  Why not the others E True papilloedema has no calcific opacities on CT.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Obese + headache + bilateral disc swelling with enlarged blind spots = papilloedema (consider idiopathic intracranial hypertension).  Why not the others A Drusen are calcified on CT and not associated with headache/raised ICP.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: A pale disc after resolved optic neuritis = optic atrophy.  Why not the others E Papilloedema is acute swelling, not chronic pallor.

choices.label: M
choices.explanation: Raised IOP with an enlarged cup-to-disc ratio = glaucomatous optic disc cupping.  Why not the others E Papilloedema fills in the cup rather than enlarging it.

questions.keyPoint: High IOP + enlarged cup = glaucomatous cupping.
questions.keyDifferentiator: 'Disc swelling' + RADIO-OPAQUE (calcified) discs on CT = drusen (pseudopapilloedema); prior optic neuritis (MS) with pale disc = optic atrophy; high IOP + glaucomatous disc = cupping; obese + headache + enlarged blind spot = papilloedema.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old TALL MYOPIC man: flashing lights + floaters + RETINAL DETACHMENT; lens DISLOCATED SUPEROTEMPORALLY in either eye.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Leukocoria + a calcifying retinal mass in a toddler = retinoblastoma.  Why not the others H Aniridia is absence of the iris, not a retinal mass.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Lisch (iris) nodules + cutaneous neurofibromas + choroidal naevi = neurofibromatosis type 1.  Why not the others E NF2 features bilateral acoustic neuromas, not Lisch nodules.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Superotemporal (up-and-out) ectopia lentis in a tall myope = Marfan syndrome (retinal-detachment risk).  Why not the others L Homocystinuria dislocates the lens DOWN and in.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Night blindness + bone-spicule pigmentation + tunnel vision, with deafness, = retinitis pigmentosa (Usher syndrome).  Why not the others J Choroideraemia is X-linked with diffuse choroidal atrophy, not the deafness association.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Tall myope + SUPEROTEMPORAL ('up-and-out') lens dislocation = Marfan (homocystinuria dislocates DOWN-and-in); leukocoria + 'cottage-cheese' mass = retinoblastoma; Lisch nodules + neurofibromas + choroidal naevi = NF1; deafness + spicule pigment + night blindness/tunnel vision = retinitis pigmentosa (Usher).

source.paper: Ophthalmology Irfan Surgery · Irfan Surgery

questions.stem: A 59-year-old man finds READING increasingly difficult and buys ready-made reading glasses.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A blood level in the AC = hyphaema.  Why not the others D Hypopyon is a pus level.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A Meibomian gland cyst/granuloma = chalazion.  Why not the others G Blepharitis is lid-margin inflammation, not a cyst.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A layer of inflammatory/pus cells in the AC = hypopyon.  Why not the others A Hyphaema is a blood level.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Age-related loss of accommodation = presbyopia.  Why not the others F Hypermetropia is long-sightedness present earlier in life.

questions.keyDifferentiator: PUS level in AC = hypopyon; BLOOD level in AC = hyphaema; age-related near difficulty = presbyopia; Meibomian cyst = chalazion; lid-margin inflammation = blepharitis.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman with SARCOIDOSIS: photophobia, blurred vision, eye pain; circumcorneal redness, AC cells, the PUPIL STUCK to the lens, small pupil.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Mid-dilated fixed oval pupil + headache/vomiting in a hyperope = acute angle-closure glaucoma.  Why not the others G Uveitis gives a small/irregular pupil.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Bilateral sticky discharge with morning crusting + normal VA = bacterial conjunctivitis.  Why not the others G Uveitis reduces VA with photophobia.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Posterior synechiae (pupil adherent to lens) + AC cells + circumcorneal injection, with sarcoid = anterior uveitis.  Why not the others E Conjunctivitis spares the AC and pupil.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Localised, sectoral, asymptomatic redness = episcleritis.  Why not the others I Scleritis is severe, boring pain.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Circumcorneal injection + AC cells + synechiae (pupil stuck to lens) = anterior uveitis; mid-dilated fixed oval pupil + nausea = angle closure; sticky morning crust = bacterial conjunctivitis; localised asymptomatic redness = episcleritis; painless red patch after coughing = subconjunctival haemorrhage.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old woman: progressive poor vision; on attempted red reflex the GP sees ALL WHITE.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A white (absent) red reflex with gradual loss = (mature) cataract.  Why not the others C AMD distorts central vision but the red reflex is preserved.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Sudden painless loss + pale retina + RAPD = (central) retinal artery occlusion.  Why not the others H Amaurosis fugax resolves; this is persistent.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Subacute loss + red desaturation + pain on eye movement = optic neuritis.  Why not the others A Cataract is painless and gradual.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Transient monocular loss that fully resolves (embolic, AF) = amaurosis fugax.  Why not the others D Retinal artery occlusion does not spontaneously resolve.

questions.keyDifferentiator: White (absent) red reflex = cataract; pale retina + sudden loss + RAPD = retinal artery occlusion; transient loss that fully resolves (AF/emboli) = amaurosis fugax; painful eye movements + dull colours = optic neuritis; angle closure can be precipitated by anticholinergics (hyoscine).

source.paper: SWM Ophthalmology (Ch13) · SWM Ch13

questions.stem: A 41-year-old woman with SJÖGREN'S syndrome and DRY EYES.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CMV retinitis in AIDS is treated with ganciclovir.  Why not the others C Aciclovir covers HSV/VZV, not CMV.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Fluorescein stains a dendritic (HSV) ulcer for diagnosis.  Why not the others C Aciclovir treats it; fluorescein diagnoses it.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Tropicamide is a short-acting mydriatic to dilate for fundoscopy.  Why not the others F Pilocarpine constricts the pupil.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Artificial tears (hypromellose) for aqueous-deficient dry eye.  Why not the others D Timolol lowers IOP, not for dry eye.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Dry eyes = hypromellose (artificial tears); dilate for fundoscopy = tropicamide; stain a dendritic/corneal ulcer = fluorescein; CMV retinitis = ganciclovir; bacterial conjunctivitis = chloramphenicol.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man: a small red swelling originating from an EYELASH FOLLICLE.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: In-turning of the lid margin with lashes abrading the cornea = entropion.  Why not the others H Ectropion turns the lid OUT (watering/exposure).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: An acutely red, swollen, infected lid — assess for proptosis/painful movements/visual loss to separate orbital from preseptal cellulitis.  Why not the others I A stye is a focal lash-follicle abscess, not diffuse lid swelling.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A yellowish degenerative conjunctival nodule (nasal) that does not cross the limbus = pinguecula.  Why not the others E BCC is a lid-margin malignancy, not a conjunctival nodule.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: An acute tender lid-margin abscess of a lash follicle = stye (external hordeolum).  Why not the others C A chalazion is a deeper, non-tender Meibomian cyst.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Tender lump on an eyelash follicle = stye; yellow conjunctival nodule near the nose = pinguecula; lower lid turned IN with lashes rubbing = entropion; Graves' + sore eyes on waking = lid retraction (exposure).

questions.stem: A 43-year-old woman with GRAVES' disease: eyes particularly SORE, especially when she WAKES in the morning.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: In-turning of the lid margin with lashes abrading the cornea = entropion.  Why not the others H Ectropion turns the lid OUT (watering/exposure).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: An acutely red, swollen, infected lid — assess for proptosis/painful movements/visual loss to separate orbital from preseptal cellulitis.  Why not the others I A stye is a focal lash-follicle abscess, not diffuse lid swelling.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A yellowish degenerative conjunctival nodule (nasal) that does not cross the limbus = pinguecula.  Why not the others E BCC is a lid-margin malignancy, not a conjunctival nodule.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Graves' lid retraction → incomplete lid closure overnight (nocturnal lagophthalmos) → corneal exposure → sore/gritty eyes worst on waking.  Why not the others I A stye is an unrelated focal lid abscess.

questions.keyPoint: Graves' + sore on waking = lid retraction (exposure).
questions.keyDifferentiator: Tender lump on an eyelash follicle = stye; yellow conjunctival nodule near the nose = pinguecula; lower lid turned IN with lashes rubbing = entropion; Graves' + sore eyes on waking = lid retraction (exposure).

flag.note: Your source sheet marked 'I' (stye) here, but a stye does not fit Graves' disease. The exam answer is LID RETRACTION (J): thyroid lid retraction causes nocturnal lagophthalmos and corneal exposure, so the eyes are sorest on waking.

questions.stem: A LEFT TEMPORAL lobe lesion.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Bilateral occipital (cortical) damage → complete cortical blindness.  Why not the others B Bitemporal loss is chiasmal, not occipital.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Chiasmal compression by a pituitary tumour → bitemporal hemianopia.  Why not the others C Homonymous defects are retrochiasmal.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A right retrochiasmal (optic tract) lesion → left homonymous hemianopia.  Why not the others G A LEFT tract lesion gives a RIGHT homonymous hemianopia.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Meyer's loop (temporal) carries superior fibres → contralateral (right) SUPERIOR quadrantanopia (PITS).  Why not the others I An inferior quadrantanopia is a parietal lesion.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Temporal lobe → contralateral SUPERIOR quadrantanopia; optic tract → contralateral homonymous hemianopia; chiasm/pituitary → bitemporal; bilateral occipital → cortical blindness; macular disease → central scotoma of that eye (PITS).

questions.stem: IDIOPATHIC INTRACRANIAL HYPERTENSION.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: AV nipping is an early hypertensive retinopathy sign (arteriolar narrowing crossing veins).  Why not the others E Microaneurysms typify diabetic, not early hypertensive, change.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Raised ICP → bilateral papilloedema.  Why not the others G A pale disc is optic atrophy, not papilloedema.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Microaneurysms are the earliest sign of background diabetic retinopathy.  Why not the others F Flame haemorrhages are typical of hypertensive retinopathy.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Proliferative (advanced) diabetic retinopathy → fibrovascular traction → tractional retinal detachment.  Why not the others E Microaneurysms are the EARLY, not advanced, sign.

questions.keyDifferentiator: IIH → papilloedema; background DR → microaneurysms (earliest sign); Marfan → lens dislocation; early hypertensive retinopathy → AV nipping; advanced (proliferative) DR → tractional retinal detachment.

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q1

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman: 1-day sudden painless loss (left), SEVERE left headache, unwell, 4 kg weight loss; left OPTIC DISC SWELLING + flame haemorrhages + cotton-wool spots.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Headache + systemic upset + weight loss + pale swollen disc in an elderly patient = arteritic AION (giant cell arteritis) — start steroids, check ESR/CRP.  Why not the others C CRAO gives a pale retina with a cherry-red spot, not disc swelling.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Pale oedematous retina + cherry-red fovea + sudden profound loss = central retinal artery occlusion (embolic).  Why not the others D CRVO has haemorrhages and tortuous veins, not a pale retina.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Disc swelling + dilated tortuous veins + widespread flame haemorrhages ('blood-and-thunder') = central retinal vein occlusion.  Why not the others C CRAO produces a pale retina, not haemorrhages.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Flashes + floaters + a descending curtain/shadow = retinal detachment.  Why not the others J Vitreous haemorrhage clouds vision but lacks the photopsia + curtain pattern.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Headache + jaw/scalp + weight loss + disc swelling = AION (GCA); cherry-red spot + pale retina = CRAO; no fundal view + absent red reflex in proliferative DR = vitreous haemorrhage; disc swelling + tortuous dilated veins + flame haemorrhages = CRVO; flashes/floaters + curtain = retinal detachment.

choices.explanation: Flashes + floaters + a descending curtain/shadow = retinal detachment.  Why not the others J Vitreous haemorrhage clouds vision but lacks the photopsia + curtain pattern.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Headache + jaw/scalp + weight loss + disc swelling = AION (GCA); cherry-red spot + pale retina = CRAO; no fundal view + absent red reflex in proliferative DR = vitreous haemorrhage; disc swelling + tortuous dilated veins + flame haemorrhages = CRVO; flashes/floaters + curtain = retinal detachment.

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q2

questions.stem: A 41-year-old woman: nausea/vomiting/abdominal pain + severe FRONTAL headache + right eye pain 'worse than labour'; right cornea CLOUDY, right pupil UNREACTIVE; VA 6/60 right.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cloudy (oedematous) cornea + fixed unreactive pupil + headache/vomiting = acute angle-closure glaucoma.  Why not the others B Uveitis gives a small reactive/irregular pupil, not a cloudy cornea with vomiting.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Photophobia + small pupil + erythema nodosum/cough (sarcoidosis) = anterior uveitis.  Why not the others C Conjunctivitis has no photophobia or AC involvement.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Rapidly worsening pain + hypopyon days after intraocular surgery = endophthalmitis.  Why not the others D Keratitis is corneal, without post-op hypopyon.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Pain on eye movement + central scotoma + RAPD with a normal fundus = (retrobulbar) optic neuritis.  Why not the others A Angle closure has a red eye with a cloudy cornea, not a normal fundus.

questions.keyPoint: Cloudy cornea + fixed pupil + vomiting = acute angle closure.

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q3

questions.stem: A 56-year-old CONTACT-LENS wearer: very painful photophobic watery red eye; multiple small white STROMAL opacities; a small irregular lesion STAINS GREEN.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Contact-lens use + corneal stromal infiltrate + an epithelial defect (stains with fluorescein) = bacterial (microbial) keratitis.  Why not the others E HSV keratitis gives a dendritic, not a round stromal, infiltrate.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A dendritic ulcer with terminal bulbs (worse in immunosuppression) = HSV keratitis (avoid steroids).  Why not the others C Bacterial keratitis gives a round infiltrate, not a dendrite.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: A V1-dermatome vesicular rash respecting the midline + ocular inflammation = herpes zoster ophthalmicus (watch for Hutchinson's sign).  Why not the others E HSV keratitis lacks a dermatomal forehead rash.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Proptosis + painful restricted eye movements + fever = orbital cellulitis (an emergency — IV antibiotics, image the orbit/sinuses).  Why not the others B Conjunctivitis has no proptosis or restricted movements.

questions.keyPoint: Contact lens + infiltrate + stained defect = bacterial keratitis.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Contact lens + stromal infiltrate + epithelial defect = bacterial keratitis; immunosuppressed + DENDRITIC ulcer = HSV keratitis; V1 vesicular rash to the midline = herpes zoster ophthalmicus; blepharitis + PERIPHERAL infiltrates + intact epithelium = marginal keratitis; proptosis + painful eye movements + fever = orbital cellulitis.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old woman: red sticky eyes with GREEN discharge on a background of 3 years of grittiness; eyes always DRY; CLUMPED lashes with CRUSTS and inflamed lids.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Watery + contagious + self-limiting = VIRAL conjunctivitis → hygiene/infection-control advice; antibiotics are not indicated.  Why not the others H Antibiotics don't treat viral conjunctivitis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Ophthalmia neonatorum needs an urgent conjunctival swab (gonococcus/chlamydia) plus antibiotics.  Why not the others A Neonatal conjunctivitis is notifiable/sight-threatening — swab + treat, don't just advise.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lid hygiene, artificial tears and topical antibiotics
choices.explanation: Chronic blepharitis + dry eye + secondary bacterial infection → lid hygiene + artificial tears + topical antibiotics.  Why not the others C Without the dry-eye component you'd omit artificial tears.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Scleritis (severe deep pain, associated with connective-tissue disease) → oral NSAIDs first-line (steroids if refractory).  Why not the others G Systemic steroids are escalation if NSAIDs fail.

choices.label: J
choices.text: Topical antihistamines and mast cell stabilisers

questions.keyPoint: Blepharitis + dry + infection = lid hygiene + tears + antibiotics.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Chronic blepharitis + dry eye + acute infection = lid hygiene + artificial tears + topical antibiotics; scleritis = oral NSAIDs; VIRAL conjunctivitis (watery, contagious) = infection-control advice (NOT antibiotics); seasonal allergic = antihistamine + mast-cell stabiliser; NEONATAL conjunctivitis = urgent swab + topical antibiotics.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old: diplopia worse on UPGAZE, lacrimation, red gritty eyes, bilateral conjunctival oedema + PROPTOSIS, 5 kg loss despite good appetite; CT shows OCULAR MUSCLE THICKENING.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A painful Horner's syndrome with neck pain = carotid artery dissection.  Why not the others J A PCom aneurysm gives a dilated pupil, not miosis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Pulsatile red eye + ocular bruit after trauma = carotid-cavernous sinus fistula.  Why not the others I Orbital cellulitis has fever and no bruit.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Proptosis + restrictive diplopia + extraocular muscle thickening + hyperthyroid features = Graves' ophthalmopathy.  Why not the others G Ocular myositis is usually unilateral/painful without thyroid features.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Fatiguable, variable diplopia/ptosis improving with ice (ice-pack test) = myasthenia gravis.  Why not the others D Graves' is restrictive and not fatiguable.

questions.keyPoint: Upgaze diplopia + proptosis + muscle thickening = Graves'.
