questions.keyDifferentiator: Diplopia worse on UPGAZE + proptosis + muscle thickening + weight loss = Graves'; ocular BRUIT after trauma = carotid-cavernous fistula; PAINFUL PUPIL-INVOLVING III palsy = PCom aneurysm; NECK PAIN + Horner's = carotid dissection; FATIGUABLE ptosis + positive ICE test = myasthenia.

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q5

questions.stem: A 54-year-old: diplopia that COMES AND GOES through the day; eyelids droop towards the END of the day; bilateral ptosis RESOLVES after an ICE PACK.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A painful Horner's syndrome with neck pain = carotid artery dissection.  Why not the others J A PCom aneurysm gives a dilated pupil, not miosis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Pulsatile red eye + ocular bruit after trauma = carotid-cavernous sinus fistula.  Why not the others I Orbital cellulitis has fever and no bruit.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Proptosis + restrictive diplopia + extraocular muscle thickening + hyperthyroid features = Graves' ophthalmopathy.  Why not the others G Ocular myositis is usually unilateral/painful without thyroid features.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Fatiguable, variable diplopia/ptosis improving with ice (ice-pack test) = myasthenia gravis.  Why not the others D Graves' is restrictive and not fatiguable.

questions.keyPoint: Fatiguable ptosis + positive ice test = myasthenia.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Diplopia worse on UPGAZE + proptosis + muscle thickening + weight loss = Graves'; ocular BRUIT after trauma = carotid-cavernous fistula; PAINFUL PUPIL-INVOLVING III palsy = PCom aneurysm; NECK PAIN + Horner's = carotid dissection; FATIGUABLE ptosis + positive ICE test = myasthenia.

questions.stem: A TOPICAL agent used to REDUCE the formation of aqueous humour in acute angle-closure glaucoma.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: HSV dendritic ulcer → topical/systemic aciclovir; AVOID steroids (worsen the ulcer).  Why not the others C Adding prednisolone to an active dendritic ulcer is harmful.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Herpes zoster ophthalmicus → systemic aciclovir (± steroid for associated inflammation/uveitis).  Why not the others B Aciclovir alone is the answer for the HSV dendritic ulcer.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: (Laser peripheral) iridotomy definitively relieves pupil block in angle closure.  Why not the others I Timolol is temporising medical therapy, not definitive.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Topical timolol (a beta-blocker) reduces aqueous production to lower IOP.  Why not the others G Pilocarpine opens the angle by miosis; it doesn't reduce aqueous formation.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Topical agent that REDUCES AQUEOUS FORMATION = timolol (beta-blocker); DEFINITIVE cure of angle closure = (laser) iridotomy; uveitis pain/photophobia = cycloplegic (cyclopentolate); HSV dendritic ulcer = aciclovir (NO steroid); zoster ophthalmicus = aciclovir + prednisolone.

questions.stem: A term for LONG-SIGHTED vision.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Developmental reduced acuity without organic pathology = amblyopia ('lazy eye').  Why not the others O Strabismus is ocular misalignment, a cause of amblyopia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Unequal corneal curvature = astigmatism.  Why not the others H Hypermetropia is an axial/refractive length issue, not corneal asphericity.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Aqueous protein scatters the beam = flare (a sign of anterior-chamber inflammation).  Why not the others J Hypopyon is a visible pus level, not beam scatter.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Long-sightedness = hypermetropia.  Why not the others K Myopia is short-sightedness.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Long-sighted = hypermetropia; reduced VA with NO organic cause = amblyopia; visible slit-lamp beam from AC protein = flare; non-spherical cornea = astigmatism; WHITE AC level = hypopyon (vs hyphaema = blood).

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q8

questions.stem: The MAJOR REFRACTIVE structure of the eye.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The ciliary body (ciliary processes) secretes aqueous humour.  Why not the others I The trabecular meshwork DRAINS aqueous, it doesn't form it.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The cornea provides ~two-thirds of the eye's refractive power.  Why not the others D The lens fine-tunes (accommodates) but is not the major refractor.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: During accommodation the ciliary muscle contracts, the zonules relax, and the LENS becomes more convex — increasing its refractive power.  Why not the others J The zonules RELAX (they don't increase power).

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Müller's (superior tarsal) muscle is sympathetically innervated — its loss causes the partial ptosis of Horner's.  Why not the others E Levator is somatic (CN III).

questions.keyDifferentiator: Major refractive surface = cornea; CN VII (eye CLOSURE) = orbicularis oculi; SYMPATHETIC lid elevation = Müller's muscle; aqueous PRODUCTION = ciliary body; accommodative refractive change = lens.

questions.topic: Q9 — Features of Chronic Ocular Disorders
questions.optionsTitle: Q9 — Features of Chronic Ocular Disorders

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q9

questions.stem: A 71-year-old: blurry vision, METAMORPHOPSIA on Amsler grid (left). Characteristic field defect?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Retinitis pigmentosa → a ring (annular mid-peripheral) scotoma progressing to tunnel vision.  Why not the others C Central vision is preserved until late in RP.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Glaucomatous nerve-fibre loss → arcuate scotoma (and nasal step).  Why not the others C Central scotoma is maculopathy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Macular degeneration (metamorphopsia) → central scotoma.  Why not the others B Arcuate scotoma is glaucomatous.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Cataract scatters light (glare) and reduces/obscures the red reflex.  Why not the others C Central scotoma is a macular field defect, not a lens problem.

questions.keyDifferentiator: AMD (metamorphopsia/drusen) → central scotoma; glaucoma (disc cupping) → arcuate scotoma; retinitis pigmentosa (night blindness, bone-spicule, reduced ERG) → annular mid-peripheral (ring) scotoma; cataract (glare) → loss of red reflex.

questions.topic: Q10 — Management of Chronic Ocular Disorders
questions.optionsTitle: Q10 — Management of Chronic Ocular Disorders

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q10

questions.stem: A 60-year-old: RAISED IOP, asymptomatic; HYPERTENSION, ischaemic heart disease, CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE and PSORIASIS; disc cupping + inferior rim notching (POAG).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Primary open-angle glaucoma needs IOP-lowering, but beta-blockers are contraindicated here (heart failure, IHD, psoriasis). A topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitor (dorzolamide) lowers IOP without those risks. (Current NICE also offers 360° selective laser trabeculoplasty first-line.)  Why not the others I Timolol is a beta-blocker — contraindicated by heart failure/IHD and can worsen psoriasis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Diabetic macular oedema → focal/grid laser photocoagulation (anti-VEGF also used).  Why not the others E Panretinal laser targets peripheral ischaemia/new vessels, not the macula.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Proliferative diabetic retinopathy (new vessels) → panretinal photocoagulation (± anti-VEGF).  Why not the others C Focal laser is for macular oedema, not peripheral neovascularisation.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Wet (neovascular) AMD → intravitreal anti-VEGF (ranibizumab) first-line.  Why not the others F Photodynamic therapy is now a second-line option.

questions.keyPoint: POAG + heart failure/psoriasis → avoid timolol; use a CAI (dorzolamide).
questions.keyDifferentiator: POAG first-line is topical drops — but AVOID beta-blockers (timolol) in heart failure/asthma/psoriasis → use a CAI (dorzolamide); proliferative DR = panretinal laser; diabetic MACULAR OEDEMA = focal laser; WET AMD = anti-VEGF (ranibizumab); failed medical glaucoma = trabeculectomy.

flag.note: Your sheet marked I (timolol). Even TOPICAL timolol is systemically absorbed and is contraindicated in congestive heart failure / IHD (bradycardia, heart block, bronchospasm) and can worsen psoriasis. First-line here is a non-beta-blocker — dorzolamide (CAI), or selective laser trabeculoplasty per current NICE.
flag.source: NICE NG81 Glaucoma; BNF — topical beta-blocker cautions

questions.stem: A 64-year-old: RETINAL NEOVASCULARISATION, flame haemorrhages, cotton-wool spots (proliferative diabetic retinopathy); no field loss.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Primary open-angle glaucoma needs IOP-lowering, but beta-blockers are contraindicated here (heart failure, IHD, psoriasis). A topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitor (dorzolamide) lowers IOP without those risks. (Current NICE also offers 360° selective laser trabeculoplasty first-line.)  Why not the others I Timolol is a beta-blocker — contraindicated by heart failure/IHD and can worsen psoriasis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Diabetic macular oedema → focal/grid laser photocoagulation (anti-VEGF also used).  Why not the others E Panretinal laser targets peripheral ischaemia/new vessels, not the macula.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Proliferative diabetic retinopathy (new vessels) → panretinal photocoagulation (± anti-VEGF).  Why not the others C Focal laser is for macular oedema, not peripheral neovascularisation.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Wet (neovascular) AMD → intravitreal anti-VEGF (ranibizumab) first-line.  Why not the others F Photodynamic therapy is now a second-line option.

questions.keyDifferentiator: POAG first-line is topical drops — but AVOID beta-blockers (timolol) in heart failure/asthma/psoriasis → use a CAI (dorzolamide); proliferative DR = panretinal laser; diabetic MACULAR OEDEMA = focal laser; WET AMD = anti-VEGF (ranibizumab); failed medical glaucoma = trabeculectomy.

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q11

questions.stem: Used to measure VISUAL ACUITY.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Biometry measures axial length/keratometry to calculate IOL power.  Why not the others F Keratometry alone gives corneal curvature, not the full IOL calculation.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A gonioscopy lens visualises the drainage angle (open vs closed).  Why not the others J Tonometry measures IOP, not the angle.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: LogMAR charts quantify visual acuity.  Why not the others H Perimetry maps fields, not acuity.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Perimetry plots the visual field.  Why not the others A Amsler grid screens the central field only.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Visual acuity = LogMAR; visual fields = perimetry; intraocular-lens power before cataract surgery = biometry; the anterior-chamber ANGLE = gonioscopy; a corneal LEAK = Seidel (colour = Ishihara; IOP = tonometry).

questions.topic: Q12 — Ocular Manifestations of Vascular Diseases
questions.optionsTitle: Q12 — Ocular Manifestations of Vascular Diseases

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q12

questions.stem: A 68-year-old (BP 170/95, glucose 10): prominent INTRARETINAL MICROVASCULAR ABNORMALITIES (IRMA), intraretinal haemorrhages and cotton-wool spots.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Reduced central vision with an unremarkable peripheral fundus = diabetic maculopathy (macular oedema not seen on direct ophthalmoscopy).  Why not the others G Mild NPDR has visible microaneurysms and doesn't drop vision.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Haemorrhages + cotton-wool spots PLUS bilateral optic disc swelling (papilloedema) with malignant BP = grade 4 hypertensive retinopathy.  Why not the others E Grade 3 has haemorrhages/exudates/cotton-wool spots but NO disc swelling.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: IRMA + extensive haemorrhages/cotton-wool spots = severe NPDR (high risk of progression to proliferative).  Why not the others G Mild NPDR is microaneurysms only.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: 'Sea-fan' peripheral neovascularisation is characteristic of proliferative sickle cell retinopathy.  Why not the others I Severe NPDR shows IRMA, not sea-fan fronds.

questions.keyDifferentiator: IRMA = SEVERE NPDR; AV nipping/broad light reflex = grade 2 HTN retinopathy; reduced vision with a near-normal fundus = diabetic maculopathy; OPTIC DISC SWELLING + malignant BP = grade 4 HTN retinopathy; 'SEA-FAN' neovascularisation = sickle cell retinopathy.

flag.note: Your sheet marked G (mild non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy), but mild NPDR is microaneurysms only. This vignette — BP 195/100 with flame haemorrhages, cotton-wool spots and bilateral OPTIC DISC SWELLING — is grade 4 (malignant) hypertensive retinopathy (Keith–Wagener–Barker).

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q13

questions.stem: The COMMONEST cause of registered blindness in the WESTERN world.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: UK driving requires ≥ 6/12 acuity (and reading a number plate at 20 m).  Why not the others C 6/60 is far below the driving standard.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: With a full field, sight impairment is registered below ~6/60 (severe sight impairment is < 3/60).  Why not the others D 3/60 is the severe sight-impairment (blind) threshold.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: AMD is the leading cause of registrable blindness in developed countries.  Why not the others J Uncorrected refractive error is the worldwide leader, not the Western one.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Uncorrected refractive error is the commonest cause of visual impairment globally.  Why not the others E AMD leads in the West, not worldwide.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Commonest blindness in the WEST = AMD; commonest visual impairment WORLDWIDE = uncorrected refractive errors; sight-impaired threshold (full field) = 6/60; UK car-driving minimum = 6/12; low-vision aid = Galilean telescope.

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q14

questions.stem: A 40-year-old (HIV): blurring + DARK SPOTS; a WHITE EXUDATIVE region nasal to the macula with large FLAME-SHAPED haemorrhages.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The 'pizza-pie' / 'cheese-and-ketchup' fundus (exudate + haemorrhage) in advanced HIV = CMV retinopathy.  Why not the others J Toxoplasma gives a focal lesion beside an old pigmented scar.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Cotton-wool spots without hypertension/diabetes in HIV = HIV microvasculopathy (retinal microvascular disease).  Why not the others E Hypertensive retinopathy needs raised BP.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: A vascular dark-red non-blanching nodule in HIV = Kaposi's sarcoma.  Why not the others H Molluscum gives pale umbilicated papules, not a dark-red nodule.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Orbital proptosis with regional lymphadenopathy in HIV = lymphoma.  Why not the others I Thyroid eye disease has no lymphadenopathy.

questions.keyDifferentiator: 'Pizza-pie' exudate + flame haemorrhages = CMV retinopathy; PIGMENTED scar + adjacent white satellite lesion = toxoplasma; cotton-wool spots with NORMAL BP/glucose = HIV microvasculopathy; dark-red NON-BLANCHING lid nodule = Kaposi's; proptosis + LYMPHADENOPATHY = lymphoma.

questions.topic: Q15 — Ocular Side Effects of Drugs
questions.optionsTitle: Q15 — Ocular Side Effects of Drugs

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q15

questions.stem: XANTHOPSIA (yellow vision).

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Amiodarone causes corneal verticillata (vortex keratopathy) and slate-grey skin.  Why not the others F Hydroxychloroquine targets the macula, not the cornea.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Steroids induce posterior subcapsular cataract and raise IOP (steroid-response glaucoma).  Why not the others D Digoxin causes xanthopsia, not cataract/glaucoma.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Digoxin toxicity classically causes xanthopsia (yellow-green vision).  Why not the others A Amiodarone causes vortex keratopathy, not xanthopsia.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Hydroxychloroquine (and chloroquine) cause a bull's-eye maculopathy — hence baseline + annual screening.  Why not the others E Ethambutol causes optic neuropathy / colour-vision loss, not bull's-eye maculopathy.

questions.keyDifferentiator: XANTHOPSIA (yellow vision) = digoxin; VORTEX keratopathy + slate-grey skin = amiodarone; CATARACT + GLAUCOMA = corticosteroids; MIOSIS = morphine/opioids; BULL'S-EYE maculopathy = hydroxychloroquine.

questions.topic: Q16 — Ocular Conditions in the Developing World
questions.optionsTitle: Q16 — Ocular Conditions in the Developing World

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q16

questions.stem: A 53-year-old (Malawi): irritable red eye, foreign-body sensation; marked TARSAL CONJUNCTIVAL SCARRING and INGROWING lashes rubbing the globe.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Trachoma (Chlamydia trachomatis): tarsal scarring + trichiasis → single-dose azithromycin (the 'A' of the SAFE strategy).  Why not the others B Face-washing is prevention; established disease needs azithromycin ± lid surgery.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Onchocerciasis ('river blindness'): snowflake keratitis + subcutaneous nodules → ivermectin.  Why not the others A Azithromycin is for trachoma.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A mature cataract (white opacity, absent red reflex) → phacoemulsification with IOL.  Why not the others J A dense cataract won't resolve spontaneously.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Choroidal tubercles in a febrile patient from a TB-endemic country = ocular tuberculosis → quadruple anti-TB therapy (RIPE).  Why not the others G Valganciclovir treats CMV retinitis, which has a haemorrhagic 'pizza-pie' picture, not choroidal tubercles.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Trachoma (tarsal scarring + trichiasis) = azithromycin; ocular TB (choroidal tubercles in an endemic patient + fever) = RIPE; onchocerciasis (snowflake keratitis + skin nodules) = ivermectin; vitamin A deficiency (night blindness + Bitot's spots) = vitamin A; mature cataract = phacoemulsification.

questions.stem: BLUE SCLERA.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Iris heterochromia (the affected iris is lighter) = congenital Horner's syndrome.  Why not the others J Wilson's affects the cornea (KF ring), not iris colour.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Corneal endothelial guttata = Fuch's endothelial dystrophy.  Why not the others A Band keratopathy is calcific, not guttata.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A Fleischer ring (iron at the base of the cone) = keratoconus.  Why not the others J A golden ring at Descemet's = Kayser–Fleischer (Wilson's), a different sign.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Thin sclera revealing the underlying uvea = blue sclera of osteogenesis imperfecta.  Why not the others J Wilson's gives a Kayser–Fleischer ring, not blue sclera.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Blue sclera = osteogenesis imperfecta; cobblestone GIANT papillae = vernal keratoconjunctivitis; corneal GUTTATA = Fuch's endothelial dystrophy; iris HETEROCHROMIA = congenital Horner's; FLEISCHER ring (iron) = keratoconus.

questions.stem: CIRCUMFERENTIAL opacity around the limbus.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Calcific interpalpebral band with 'Swiss-cheese' lucencies = band keratopathy (e.g. chronic uveitis, hypercalcaemia).  Why not the others D Arcus is a peripheral ring, not a central holey band.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A circumferential peripheral corneal ring (arcus) — in a younger patient — suggests hyperlipidaemia.  Why not the others J Wilson's gives a golden-brown Kayser–Fleischer ring, not a white lipid arcus.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Vogt's striae (vertical stress lines in the deep stroma) = keratoconus.  Why not the others C Fuch's shows guttata, not Vogt's striae.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: A triangular fibrovascular growth crossing the limbus onto the cornea = pterygium.  Why not the others H A pinguecula stays at the limbus and does NOT invade the cornea.

questions.keyDifferentiator: CIRCUMFERENTIAL limbal arcus = hyperlipidaemia (vs a golden Kayser–Fleischer ring = Wilson's); LISCH nodules = neurofibromatosis; calcific band with 'Swiss-cheese' holes = band keratopathy; TRIANGULAR growth ONTO the cornea = pterygium (pinguecula stays at the limbus); VOGT'S striae = keratoconus.

choices.explanation: Calcific interpalpebral band with 'Swiss-cheese' lucencies = band keratopathy (e.g. chronic uveitis, hypercalcaemia).  Why not the others D Arcus is a peripheral ring, not a central holey band.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A circumferential peripheral corneal ring (arcus) — in a younger patient — suggests hyperlipidaemia.  Why not the others J Wilson's gives a golden-brown Kayser–Fleischer ring, not a white lipid arcus.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Vogt's striae (vertical stress lines in the deep stroma) = keratoconus.  Why not the others C Fuch's shows guttata, not Vogt's striae.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: A triangular fibrovascular growth crossing the limbus onto the cornea = pterygium.  Why not the others H A pinguecula stays at the limbus and does NOT invade the cornea.

questions.keyDifferentiator: CIRCUMFERENTIAL limbal arcus = hyperlipidaemia (vs a golden Kayser–Fleischer ring = Wilson's); LISCH nodules = neurofibromatosis; calcific band with 'Swiss-cheese' holes = band keratopathy; TRIANGULAR growth ONTO the cornea = pterygium (pinguecula stays at the limbus); VOGT'S striae = keratoconus.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old (T1DM): profound left PTOSIS, the left eye 'DOWN AND OUT'; the pupil is NOT dilated and reacts normally to light/accommodation.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A tonic pupil that is supersensitive to dilute pilocarpine (denervation supersensitivity) = Adie's (Holmes–Adie) pupil.  Why not the others C Argyll Robertson pupils are small and irregular, not tonic/dilated.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Light-near dissociation with small irregular pupils = Argyll Robertson pupil.  Why not the others A Adie's is a large tonic pupil, not small/irregular.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: A 'down-and-out' eye with ptosis but a SPARED pupil = a (pupil-sparing) microvascular third-nerve palsy, typical of diabetes.  Why not the others D Horner's gives miosis + ptosis, not a down-and-out eye.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: A fixed, oval, poorly-reactive pupil with a severe headache points to acute angle-closure glaucoma (or an aneurysmal third-nerve palsy) — neither is one of the listed pupil syndromes.  Why not the others E Marcus Gunn is an afferent defect with a round, consensually-reacting pupil.

questions.keyPoint: Down-and-out + ptosis + pupil spared = microvascular CN III.
questions.keyDifferentiator: PUPIL-SPARING 'down-and-out' eye in a diabetic = microvascular CN III lesion; small irregular pupils + light-near dissociation = Argyll Robertson; unilateral miosis persisting in the DARK (+ syringomyelia) = Horner's; OVAL poorly-reactive pupil + severe headache = acute angle closure (None of the above); tonic pupil supersensitive to DILUTE pilocarpine = Adie's.

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q20

questions.stem: A 3-year-old: a squint present in ALL DIRECTIONS of gaze; the left eye only fixates when the right is occluded.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A squint with the same angle in all positions of gaze = concomitant squint.  Why not the others F A non-concomitant squint varies with the direction of gaze (paralytic).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A hereditary lens opacity gives leukocoria with an otherwise-normal fundus — congenital cataract needs urgent surgery to prevent stimulus-deprivation amblyopia. CRITICAL: any leukocoria must first urgently EXCLUDE retinoblastoma.  Why not the others H Retinoblastoma also causes leukocoria and must always be excluded, but it produces a retinal MASS rather than an otherwise-normal fundus.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Buphthalmos (enlarged eye + cloudy cornea), epiphora and photophobia = congenital glaucoma.  Why not the others E Nasolacrimal duct obstruction doesn't enlarge or cloud the cornea.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A watery infant eye with mucus but no infection = congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction (most resolve by 12 months).  Why not the others G Ophthalmia neonatorum is a purulent infection in the first weeks.

questions.keyPoint: Same angle in all gaze = concomitant squint.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Squint the SAME in all gaze positions = concomitant (non-concomitant varies with gaze); watery infant eye with mucus, white eye otherwise = nasolacrimal duct obstruction; premature + low birth weight + peripheral proliferative retinopathy = ROP; BUPHTHALMOS (enlarged cloudy cornea + epiphora) = congenital glaucoma; LEUKOCORIA = always exclude retinoblastoma.

questions.stem: A 33-year-old: CEMENT splashed in the eye; conjunctival injection/swelling with cement traces and mild corneal oedema.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Clean the tarsal conjunctiva and topical antibiotics
choices.explanation: Vertical linear corneal abrasions = a SUBTARSAL foreign body → evert the lid, clean the tarsal conjunctiva, give topical antibiotics.  Why not the others A You must also give antibiotics after removing the FB.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A retrobulbar haemorrhage / orbital compartment syndrome (proptosis + high IOP + RAPD) needs emergency lateral canthotomy and cantholysis to decompress the orbit.  Why not the others E Sight is threatened — observation would lose the eye.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: A chemical (alkali) burn needs immediate copious irrigation, then topical antibiotics.  Why not the others E Observation risks irreversible alkali penetration.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: A corneal abrasion (arc-eye/photokeratitis) → topical antibiotic + padding + analgesia.  Why not the others D Imaging found no FB; this is a surface abrasion.

choices.label: J
choices.text: Topical steroids, topical antibiotics and padding

questions.keyDifferentiator: Chemical (cement/alkali) burn = immediate IRRIGATION; retrobulbar haemorrhage (proptosis + raised IOP + RAPD) = emergency lateral CANTHOTOMY/cantholysis; blowout fracture (vertical diplopia + cheek numbness) = surgical reduction; corneal abrasion = topical antibiotic + pad + analgesia; VERTICAL corneal scratches = subtarsal foreign body (evert + clean tarsal conjunctiva).

questions.stem: A school bus driver becoming LONG-SIGHTED who needs spectacles.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Hypermetropia is corrected with a convex (converging/plus) lens.  Why not the others I A toric lens corrects astigmatism, not simple long-sight.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Creating a stromal flap then ablating the bed = LASIK.  Why not the others F PRK ablates the surface without a flap.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: A soft contact lens corrects refraction and doubles as a bandage lens for epithelial defects.  Why not the others C A hard lens is not used as a bandage.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Astigmatism is corrected with a toric lens.  Why not the others B A convex lens corrects long-sight, not astigmatism.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Long-sighted = convex (plus) lens; BANDAGE for an epithelial defect = soft contact lens; myopia + presbyopia (all distances) = varifocal; corneal stromal FLAP + ablation = LASIK; astigmatism = toric lens.

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q22

questions.stem: A MUSICIAN (known myope) who now needs spectacles for PRESBYOPIA.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Hypermetropia is corrected with a convex (converging/plus) lens.  Why not the others I A toric lens corrects astigmatism, not simple long-sight.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Creating a stromal flap then ablating the bed = LASIK.  Why not the others F PRK ablates the surface without a flap.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: A soft contact lens corrects refraction and doubles as a bandage lens for epithelial defects.  Why not the others C A hard lens is not used as a bandage.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Combined myopia + presbyopia needing seamless distance–intermediate–near (e.g. reading music) → varifocal (progressive) lens.  Why not the others A Bifocals lack the intermediate zone useful at a music stand.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Long-sighted = convex (plus) lens; BANDAGE for an epithelial defect = soft contact lens; myopia + presbyopia (all distances) = varifocal; corneal stromal FLAP + ablation = LASIK; astigmatism = toric lens.

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q23

questions.stem: Bitemporal hemianopia.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: An altitudinal field defect (a horizontal 'shelf') = anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy.  Why not the others B Glaucoma gives arcuate defects/tunnel vision, not a single altitudinal shelf.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Advanced chronic open-angle glaucoma produces progressive peripheral loss → tunnel vision.  Why not the others F Papilloedema doesn't typically cause symmetrical tunnel vision.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Bitemporal hemianopia = compression of the optic chiasm.  Why not the others E An optic tract lesion gives a homonymous defect.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Papilloedema classically ENLARGES THE BLIND SPOT; with chronic papilloedema and secondary optic atrophy the central field is involved, giving (centro)central scotomas.  Why not the others D A chiasm lesion gives bitemporal, not central, loss.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Bitemporal hemianopia = chiasm; bilateral tunnel vision = advanced chronic open-angle glaucoma; superior quadrantanopia = temporal lobe (PITS); unilateral altitudinal loss = anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy.

source.paper: Master Pass Ophthalmology · Master Pass Q23

questions.stem: A 22-year-old: TWO small PEARLY lesions with UMBILICATED centres on the upper lid margin; chronic irritable eye.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A translucent, fluid-filled cyst on the anterior lid margin = cyst of Moll (an apocrine sweat-gland cyst).  Why not the others B A chalazion is a deep, firm, painless Meibomian-gland granuloma in the tarsal plate — not a translucent marginal cyst.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: An opaque marginal cyst (sebaceous gland of Zeis) with a normal tarsal conjunctiva on eversion = cyst of Zeis.  Why not the others B A chalazion would be visible on eversion as a tarsal nodule.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: An acutely painful, tender Meibomian-gland abscess (often an infected chalazion) = internal hordeolum.  Why not the others B A chalazion is chronic and painless.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Pearly umbilicated papules (± secondary follicular conjunctivitis) = molluscum contagiosum.  Why not the others D A cyst of Zeis is a single opaque marginal cyst, not pearly/umbilicated.

questions.keyDifferentiator: Pearly UMBILICATED papules = molluscum; TRANSLUCENT fluid cyst on the anterior margin = cyst of Moll (apocrine); OPAQUE cyst = cyst of Zeis (sebaceous); painful tender Meibomian lump = internal hordeolum; rapid growth then INVOLUTION with a central keratin crater = keratoacanthoma.

flag.note: Your sheet marked B (chalazion). A chalazion is a deep, firm Meibomian-gland granuloma. A TRANSLUCENT, fluid-filled cyst on the ANTERIOR lid margin = cyst of Moll (apocrine). Contrast with Case 3's OPAQUE cyst of Zeis (sebaceous) — the classic Moll-vs-Zeis translucent/opaque distinction.

questions.stem: An 83-year-old: sudden right visual loss + 3 weeks of SCALP TENDERNESS; pale oedematous disc — suspected cranial arteritis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Measure BP, random glucose and full blood count
choices.explanation: CRVO work-up screens for hypertension, diabetes and hyperviscosity (BP, glucose, FBC).  Why not the others J Carotid duplex is for arterial (CRAO) emboli, not venous occlusion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No investigations — contact the duty ophthalmologist
choices.explanation: Acute angle-closure glaucoma is a sight-threatening emergency — contact ophthalmology immediately to lower the IOP.  Why not the others D Imaging delays sight-saving treatment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Arrange urgent CT scan of the orbits

choices.label: E
choices.text: Send blood for ESR and CRP
choices.explanation: Giant cell arteritis → urgent ESR + CRP (and start steroids); confirm with temporal artery biopsy.  Why not the others B Vascular screen bloods don't capture the inflammatory markers GCA needs.

choices.label: G
choices.text: Send blood for connective tissue diseases screen

choices.label: H
choices.text: Discuss urgent MR angiogram with the duty neuroradiologist
choices.explanation: A painful, pupil-involving III palsy needs urgent angiography (MR/CT angiogram) to exclude a posterior communicating artery aneurysm.  Why not the others A Skull X-rays won't show an aneurysm.

choices.label: I
choices.text: Request urgent ophthalmic artery perfusion by Doppler

choices.label: J
choices.text: Measure BP, glucose and request carotid duplex ultrasound

questions.keyDifferentiator: GCA = ESR + CRP; pupil-involving III palsy = MR angiogram (aneurysm); acute angle closure = contact ophthalmology NOW; CRVO = BP/glucose/FBC; CRAO = carotid duplex (embolic source).

source.paper: The Eye & Orbit (Ch41) · Ch41 Theme 2

questions.stem: A 2-year-old: acute SWELLING and REDNESS of the eyelids; the underlying eye and ORBIT appear NORMAL.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A fluctuant swelling at the medial canthus (over the lacrimal sac) = lacrimal sac mucocele.  Why not the others H A Meibomian cyst sits in the lid, not at the medial canthus.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A lid-margin molluscum lesion sheds viral particles → chronic follicular conjunctivitis (red irritable eye).  Why not the others H A Meibomian cyst doesn't cause a chronic follicular conjunctivitis.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Lid swelling with a normal globe/orbit (no proptosis or painful movements) = preseptal (periorbital) cellulitis.  Why not the others B A lacrimal sac mucocele localises to the medial canthus.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: A chronic, firm, painless lump deep in the tarsal plate = Meibomian cyst (chalazion).  Why not the others C Molluscum is a superficial pearly umbilicated papule.

questions.keyPoint: Lid swelling + normal orbit = preseptal cellulitis.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Child with lid swelling + NORMAL orbit = preseptal cellulitis; firm deep painless lid lump = Meibomian cyst; tense fluctuant swelling MEDIAL to the medial canthus = lacrimal sac mucocele; lid-margin lump + chronic red eye = molluscum; PEARLY lump + central ulcer that BLEEDS = basal cell carcinoma.

choices.explanation: A chronic, firm, painless lump deep in the tarsal plate = Meibomian cyst (chalazion).  Why not the others C Molluscum is a superficial pearly umbilicated papule.

questions.keyPoint: Firm deep painless lid lump = Meibomian cyst.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old: a TENSE, FLUCTUANT swelling just MEDIAL to the medial canthus.

source.paper: The Eye & Orbit (Ch41) · Ch41 Theme 3

questions.stem: A 23-year-old: pain + photophobia; PINK coloration around the edge of the cornea and a SMALL IRREGULAR pupil.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A flat, painless, bright-red patch with normal vision = subconjunctival haemorrhage.  Why not the others E Scleritis is deeply painful.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Circumcorneal (ciliary) flush + small irregular pupil (posterior synechiae) + photophobia = anterior uveitis.  Why not the others C Subconjunctival haemorrhage is painless with a normal pupil.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A deep-red, oedematous, painful sclera = scleritis (often associated with systemic vasculitis).  Why not the others C Subconjunctival haemorrhage is painless and flat.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Sticky discharge with crusting on waking and normal vision = bacterial conjunctivitis.  Why not the others G Adenoviral conjunctivitis is watery with follicles.

questions.keyPoint: Ciliary flush + small irregular pupil = uveitis.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Circumcorneal (ciliary) flush + small IRREGULAR pupil + photophobia = uveitis; BRANCHING (dendritic) corneal staining = HSV keratitis; painless BRIGHT-RED patch = subconjunctival haemorrhage; sticky crusted lids = bacterial conjunctivitis; DEEP red sclera + boring pain + oedema = scleritis.

source.paper: The Eye & Orbit (Ch41) · Ch41 Theme 4

questions.stem: A 23-year-old PUNCHED in the eye: eye intact but DIPLOPIA with restricted UPWARD gaze.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Instil antibiotic ointment and place an eye pad
choices.explanation: After removing a superficial corneal foreign body → antibiotic ointment + an eye pad.  Why not the others D Imaging isn't needed for a removed superficial corneal FB.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prophylactic IV antibiotics and a temporary tarsorrhaphy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Local anaesthetic and irrigation of the cornea/conjunctival sac with saline
choices.explanation: A chemical (alkali) burn → anaesthetise and IRRIGATE copiously with saline until the pH normalises.  Why not the others J You irrigate with saline/water — you do NOT neutralise an alkali with acid.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Arrange plain X-ray or CT scan of the eyes
choices.explanation: Hammering metal carries a high risk of an INTRAOCULAR foreign body → image (X-ray/CT) to locate it.  Why not the others A Padding a possible penetrating injury risks pressure on a ruptured globe.

choices.label: F
choices.text: CT scan of the facial and orbital bones, including coronal views
choices.explanation: Restricted up-gaze + diplopia after blunt trauma = orbital blowout fracture → CT facial/orbital bones with coronal views.  Why not the others E Ultrasound poorly assesses bony orbital walls.

choices.label: G
choices.text: MRI of the eyes and orbits

choices.label: H
choices.text: Examination under anaesthetic and surgical repair as required

choices.label: I
choices.text: Take a corneal scrape for bacterial culture

choices.label: J
choices.text: Irrigate with weak acetic acid until the pH is neutral

questions.keyDifferentiator: Blowout fracture (vertical diplopia + restricted upgaze) = CT facial/orbital bones (CORONAL); corneal FB after removal = antibiotic ointment + pad; HAMMERING metal (penetrating risk) = image for an INTRAOCULAR FB; alkali splash = saline irrigation; distorted pupil + shallow AC + prolapsed iris (ruptured globe) = EUA + surgical repair.
